query: What position did Jason Semore hold at Valdosta State before returning to Georgia Tech?
positive:
In the heart of North Florida, a high school legend was born. He was a quarterback like no other, dazzling fans and foes alike as he amassed over 5,300 yards and threw 59 touchdowns at North Florida Christian High School. His talent earned him a place at Florida State University, where he began his college journey. Yet, it was at West Virginia that he truly soared, leading the Big 12 in completion percentage in 2014 and earning recognition as a semifinalist for the prestigious Davey O’Brien Award.

Years later, a new chapter unfolded as Jason Semore joined the Marshall coaching staff for the 2023 college football season, stepping into the role of defensive coordinator for the Thundering Herd. His arrival in Huntington came after a season spent at Georgia Tech, a recent stop on a coaching journey that had taken him through various roles and levels of competition. 

Once a linebacker at Adams State from 2001 to 2005, Semore's path was marked by success. In 2022, he focused on guiding the Georgia Tech linebackers, with plans to take on the special teams coordinator role, overseeing nickel backs and safeties. However, fate had other plans, bringing him back to a familiar position he had held at Valdosta State, Montana, and the Colorado School of Mines, where he also served as co-defensive coordinator at his alma mater. 

In each of these roles, Semore had tasted victory. He played a key part in leading the Colorado School of Mines to a remarkable 10-2 season, assisted Montana in achieving three consecutive winning seasons alongside a top-ranked Big Sky defense, and his defensive prowess fueled Valdosta State's thrilling run to the DII title game in 2021. As he settled into his new role with the Thundering Herd, it was clear that Semore brought with him a legacy of success and an unwavering commitment to excellence.

Throughout his career, he has navigated the coaching landscape of several schools across the southeast, forging a web of recruiting connections along the way. His journey brought him to work alongside Geoff Collins during Collins' second term at Mississippi State. Together in Starkvegas, they crafted a formidable defensive front that made waves in the SEC, ranking No. 2 in sacks in 2014 with an impressive tally of 37 and clinching the No. 4 spot in rushing defense in 2013, allowing just 144.2 yards per game. As he moved to Florida, his achievements only multiplied; his defensive front soared to the top two in the SEC and ranked among the top 25 nationally in sacks for both 2019 and 2020. Beyond these remarkable statistics, he also played a pivotal role in the development of standout players who would go on to make their mark in the NFL, including Fletcher Cox, Patrick Kerney, and Myles Garrett. Now, Semore returns to Georgia Tech for the second chapter of his career, having spent a year at Valdosta State. He steps into the role of linebackers coach, succeeding Defensive Coordinator Andrew Thacker, ready to leave his mark once again.

In 2006, Semore embarked on his coaching journey at Round Valley High School in Arizona, serving as the Defensive Coordinator. The following year, he took his talents to the Colorado School of Mines as a Secondary Coach. His career continued to flourish from 2008 to 2009 at Adams State, where he held the roles of Co-Defensive Coordinator, Secondary Coach, and Special Teams Coordinator. By 2010, he found himself at Tulsa, coaching the Outside Linebackers for two years. His path then led him to Oklahoma State in 2012 and 2013, where he worked as a Defensive Assistant before returning to the Colorado School of Mines in 2014 as the Defensive Coordinator.

In 2015, Semore became the Secondary Coach and Special Teams Coordinator at Montana, a position he held until he ascended to Defensive Coordinator from 2016 to 2017. In 2018, he took on the role of Senior Defensive Advisor at Temple, followed by a stint as a Defensive Analyst at Georgia Tech from 2019 to 2020. His most recent chapter began in 2021 when he became the Defensive Coordinator at Valdosta State.

With a wealth of experience greater than I had initially realized, Semore's current position at Valdosta State presents him with new challenges. His defensive unit there achieved remarkable success, ranking in the top 20 nationally for defensive touchdowns, pass defense, red zone defense, and fumble recoveries. The Blazers even made a noteworthy appearance in the national championship game, though they ultimately returned without the coveted trophy. While Semore and his counterpart may have varying levels of experience, together they are stepping into the shoes of Andrew Thacker. Although Thacker retains the title of defensive coordinator, he no longer serves as the linebackers coach, marking a significant shift in the coaching landscape.

Stitt leaned back in his chair, recalling the first time he truly recognized Semore's potential. “He really understood everything about the defense and how it worked,” he said, a hint of admiration in his voice. “And I was like, ‘Man, this guy’s a young guy, but he’s ready to do this.’” When Stitt took on the role of head coach at FCS Montana in December 2014, he wasted no time in bringing Semore on board. First, he appointed him as safeties coach and special-teams coordinator, before promoting him to defensive coordinator. “He really relates well to the players, and he just has a way of getting the most out of them,” Stitt explained, a smile crossing his face. “They really, really play hard for him.” 

It was during his time at Oklahoma State that Gundy, not being familiar with Semore, inadvertently bestowed upon him a nickname: Boogie. “It was probably because he didn’t remember my name,” Semore chuckled, reflecting on those early days. “It was right when I got hired, and then everybody started calling me that.” His journey at Oklahoma State also intertwined him with Andrew Thacker, a graduate assistant at the time. Thacker recognized Semore’s talent and later brought him to Temple in 2018 as a defensive analyst. From there, Collins would invite him to Tech for a similar position, which he held until he accepted a defensive coordinator role at Division II Valdosta State. 

But the story of the nickname lingered in the air, as Malone, the Kansas State assistant coach who had facilitated Semore’s move from Tulsa to Oklahoma State, recalled with a laugh, “I do remember Jason always being like, ‘Damn, he doesn’t know my name’ when he first got there.”

Semore's journey in the world of football had taken him through various institutions, culminating in his last two seasons at Tech. Before that, he spent a season at Temple and three at Montana, where he honed his skills as a special teams coordinator and secondary coach in 2015, later stepping into the role of defensive coordinator for the following two seasons, from 2016 to 2017. The 2019 season saw VSU's renowned "Black Swarm" defense shining brightly, allowing only 18.55 points per game, while the offense thrived under Goff's leadership, averaging an impressive 38.9 points per contest in his inaugural season. With Semore at the helm of the defense and a seasoned group returning, Goff's anticipation for the 2021 version of the Black Swarm was palpable. “That’s exactly why I hired Coach Semore –– he has an aggressive style of calling a game,” Goff remarked, his enthusiasm evident. “I want an aggressive style defense. We’ve got to be aggressive on both sides. It’ll be calculated risks, but you’re going to see us pressuring the quarterback and striving to prevent big plays while stopping the run.” He paused, reflecting on the legacy of Valdosta State's defense. “On the defensive side, Valdosta State's been called the Black Swarm since I played here. We’re going to keep that name and we’re going to live up to that expectation.” Meanwhile, Shane Thomas, the sports editor at the Valdosta Daily Times, kept an eye on the latest developments, eager to share updates with the community.

At a certain moment, a conversation might unfold between him and Batt regarding the prospect of Key stepping into the role of head coach. Key knows that Batt's current focus is on evaluating potential coaches, while his own mission is to showcase the best possible performance on the field to strengthen his case. Even if Batt opts to hire someone from outside the program, a strong showing as the interim coach could propel Key into consideration for other head coaching positions. He is also mindful of instilling in his players that winning isn’t something to take for granted, especially after achieving victories back-to-back. Yet, he appreciates the chance for his team to revel in the renewed energy surrounding the campus. 

“It’s been a while since we had this kind of momentum in the program,” tight end Dylan Leonard shared with reporters on Monday. “It’s our first time with back-to-back wins. It’s great for us as players, but it’s also cool to see the fan base. You hear different conversations in the classroom. There’s a little more hype. It’s really cool walking around campus seeing people be excited about us.” 

Semore had previously served as an analyst at Georgia Tech during 2019 and 2020, before taking on the role of defensive coordinator at Valdosta State last season. There, he encountered a team imbued with a winning mentality, with the Blazers stepping onto the field each week with the expectation of victory. However, upon his return to Georgia Tech this season as the linebackers coach, he noted that the Yellow Jackets lacked that same confidence. But after securing a couple of wins, the spark of belief was beginning to ignite within them.

In the year 2000, Semore stepped off the high school graduation stage, ready to embark on a new chapter of his playing career at Division II Adams State in Alamosa, Colorado. As he settled into college life, he found himself chuckling whenever his coaches praised him for “living in the weight room.” To Semore, all those hours spent lifting weights back in Ganado were simply a part of his routine—after all, what else was there to do? Reflecting on his journey, he remarked, “When I got to college and I got off the reservation, I really believed that I had a different view as far as what I did with my time.” 

By the time he donned his cap and gown at Adams State, his father had taken on the role of head coach at a high school two hours south of Ganado, close to the New Mexico border. Semore eagerly stepped into the position of defensive coordinator, ready to make his mark. The following year, fortune smiled upon him as he landed his first collegiate coaching job as the secondary coach for the Division II Colorado School of Mines. From that moment on, Semore remained entrenched in the collegiate coaching landscape, meticulously navigating a variety of defensive roles.

His journey led him back to Adams State in 2007, where he took on the role of co-defensive coordinator, marking the beginning of four defensive coordinator positions he would hold throughout his career. In 2012, while working as a defensive assistant at Oklahoma State, he crossed paths with Andrew Thacker, the Tech defensive coordinator. This connection would ultimately open doors to the Georgia Tech staff. In coaching, Semore learned, success hinges not just on performance, but also on the strength of one’s network.

In the heart of Huntington, a new chapter began for the Marshall football team as Johnathan Galante took the helm as the defensive coordinator. His journey to this pivotal role was marked by a diverse coaching résumé that he had meticulously crafted over the years. Galante joined the Thundering Herd in 2022, initially stepping in as the special teams coordinator. A former outside linebacker at Virginia Tech, he first dipped his toes into coaching as a special teams graduate assistant from 2017 to 2018, where he honed his skills alongside the Hokies. After that, he spent four years sharpening his expertise as a special teams analyst for Alabama, before making his way to Marshall.

Meanwhile, Bill Legg emerged as a familiar figure within the Thundering Herd, boasting more connections to the program than anyone else on the coaching staff. This marked Legg's third stint with the school, where he took on the crucial role of offensive line coach. His history with Marshall was rich; he had previously served as the tight ends coach during two separate terms and orchestrated the offense as the offensive coordinator from 2010 to 2017. His coaching journey also led him through notable stops at West Virginia, Purdue, Mississippi State, and FIU in the FBS realm.

In the wake of these changes, Chevis Jackson found himself stepping into a significant role as well. Having previously coached the cornerbacks for the Thundering Herd, he received a promotion to co-defensive coordinator just in time for the 2023 season. Jackson’s path to Huntington had taken him from Kansas, adding to a coaching career that had already taken him to Ball State, LSU, and back to his roots with the South Alabama Jaguars, where it all began. As the new season approached, the Thundering Herd were poised for a fresh start under the guidance of their evolving coaching staff.

From an early age, Jason learned to draw parallels between life and the game of football, understanding that preparation is the key to success in both arenas. His journey began in 2005 when he earned a bachelor's degree in history, government, and philosophy from Adams State. Two years later, he furthered his education with a master's in criminal justice administration from the University of Phoenix, and in 2012, he added another master's in educational methods from the University of Tulsa. 

Throughout his career, Jason carved out a significant niche in the world of football coaching. He took on the role of defensive coordinator at the University of Montana, later becoming co-defensive coordinator at Adams State. His expertise led him to Valdosta State and the Colorado School of Mines, where he continued to hone his craft. He served as a defensive advisor at Temple University and spent two years as a defensive assistant at Oklahoma State, following a two-season stint with Tulsa as the outside linebackers coach. Notably, he guided Valdosta State to the Division II national championship game as their defensive coordinator before accepting the linebackers coaching position for the Yellow Jackets in 2012.

Looking back on his upbringing in Ganado, Jason reflects on his path with a sense of clarity. He dismisses the notion that his rise from a small, relatively unknown town to the heights of college football was daunting. “I never thought of it that way,” he admits. “I was raised not to be result-oriented in my approach. Results are a by-product of daily habits and choices. Over time, if you have success, it is ultimately because of the people you’re around and how disciplined you are with your day-in and day-out habits.”

Semore had been known as “Boogie” ever since the nickname took hold, a title he embraced despite its origins being less than flattering—bestowed upon him for sheer convenience rather than personal preference. The backstory behind the name was likely familiar to Gary Goff, the previous coach at Valdosta State, who had brought Semore on board from Tech, where he had served as a defensive analyst. Under Goff’s leadership, the Blazers made an impressive run to the Division II title game, a success that propelled Goff to the head coaching position at FCS McNeese State and paved the way for Semore’s return to Tech, this time as the linebackers coach. “Here’s what I love about Semore,” Goff remarked. “He’s a great person, but he’s in this profession for the right reason, and that’s to change lives through the sport. He doesn’t have an ego. It’s always the players and the team first, and I really appreciate that.” It was this blend of coaching prowess, readiness, and selflessness that led Key to name Semore as his new special-teams coordinator without hesitation. “He’s a ball coach,” Key declared, “and I can’t give him any higher compliment than that.” With the support of his fellow assistant coaches, Semore rose to the challenge of teaching a new punt-protection scheme on the fly, making the bold move to replace seven players from the previous unit. He understood the risks involved in changing strategies at such a critical juncture, especially given Pitt’s aggressive style of play.

As the sun dipped below the horizon, casting a warm glow over the field, the excitement in the air was palpable. "We're going to run our Air Raid offense," the coach declared, his eyes gleaming with determination. "We’re going to be who we are because that young man right there can do it all." He gestured towards the quarterback, a promising talent who had captured everyone's attention. "We've all seen him practice; the guy's got a cannon. He can throw the ball through the uprights from 70 yards away. You tell him to use that arm strength, but sometimes you don’t even have to. He’s worked really hard this summer, getting on the same page with the receivers, and it showed today. At the end of the first practice, he made some beautiful throws that left everyone in awe."

The coach's voice softened, tinged with hope. "He’s got all the talent in the world, and I want him to be himself. I’m not worried about him being anybody else – I just want him to go out there and play the game he excels at, the game he’s capable of being great at. Don’t press the issue, I told him. He’s got a lot of talent around him. Just make sure to utilize your four outstanding receivers and your two fantastic running backs, and we’re going to be just fine."

Yet, it wasn’t just the players who faced change. The Blazers had experienced some turnover in personnel, losing key members of their coaching staff during the canceled 2020 campaign. The most significant departure was defensive coordinator Lee Stalker, who had moved on to Division I Kent State, taking on the role of outside linebackers coach for the Golden Flashes. In the midst of this turmoil, Goff made a pivotal decision, tapping former Georgia Tech defensive quality control specialist Jason Semore to lead the defense, hoping to steer the team through this new chapter.

On September 29, 2022, Jason Semore found himself reflecting on his journey. Just a year prior, he had played a pivotal role as the defensive coordinator for Valdosta State, guiding the team to the NCAA Division II national championship game. It was a moment of triumph that would stay with him as he transitioned back into new challenges ahead.

On January 5, 2023, Wallace's journey took him back to familiar territory after a significant chapter at Valdosta State, where he dedicated the years from 2009 to 2013 to shaping young athletes, culminating in his final three seasons as the defensive coordinator. In 2014, he made his way back to Iowa, ready to embrace new challenges and opportunities in his coaching career.
negative:
By Andy Demetra, The Voice of the Yellow Jackets

In the world of college football, stories of young boys growing up in the shadows of their fathers, who are often coaches themselves, are as common as the playbooks they study. Such is the tale of Jason Semore, a newly minted special teams coordinator whose journey is both familiar and unique. Born and raised in the small town of Ganado, Arizona, he was the son of a coach, naturally drawn to the game. Following in his father's footsteps, he navigated the complex landscape of football, grinding his way up the coaching ladder, carving out a niche for himself along the way.

His path, however, diverges from the typical narrative of coaches’ sons. After playing under his father in high school and later earning accolades at the collegiate level, Semore embarked on a remarkable journey spanning 17 years. He hopped from Division II to FCS to FBS, traversing the country and accumulating experiences as a small-school coordinator before rising to the ranks of a Power-5 analyst. Ultimately, he landed at Georgia Tech as a linebackers coach, where his dedication and hard work were recognized with a promotion to special teams coordinator—a position he previously held at the University of Montana in 2015.

What sets Semore apart from the countless others is not merely his ascent through the coaching realm but the roots from which he sprang. The key to understanding his unique perspective lies subtly in the first line of his biography: Hometown: Ganado, Ariz. It’s a detail that many might overlook, yet it encapsulates a journey that is distinctly his own—a journey that defies the conventions of Division I coaching.

Jason Semore, a name synonymous with sports in Ganado, Arizona, has transitioned from a celebrated athlete to a devoted coach. Once a dynamic linebacker for Ganado High School's Hornets, he now imparts his wealth of football knowledge to aspiring linebackers at Georgia Tech University in Atlanta, a prestigious institution known for its research and membership in the Atlantic Coast Conference. At 40, there’s still vitality in his throwing arm, and his legs remain sprightly, yet his focus lies squarely on nurturing the next generation of players.

"I played football, baseball, track and field, and I wrestled at Ganado High School," he reminisces, recalling the glory days when he was part of the Hornets' championship-winning teams of the late '90s and early 2000s. His journey began with baseball, his first love, but football was always in his blood, thanks to his father’s coaching influence. Born in Portland, Oregon, Semore's roots run deep in the Navajo Nation, where he spent 17 formative years on the reservation, close to the grandparents who had settled in Ganado during the 1970s. 

On the field, he thrived in various roles—playing linebacker and running back in football, catching and manning third base in baseball, and sprinting in track. Yet, he found his true passion in defense, both as a player and now as a coach, where he eagerly guides young athletes to develop their skills and strategies on the field.

"My preference has always been defense, both as a player and a coach,” Semore reflected, a hint of nostalgia in his voice. “Defense feels more reactive, allowing me to play the game with emotion, and it required less thinking on my part. Offense, in contrast, is all about execution and assignments. While defense does involve those principles, it ultimately hinges on effort and technique.” He paused, recalling the man who had ignited his passion for coaching—his father, Russ. A former coach at Ganado High, Russ had returned to his alma mater after his college days and military service to embark on a coaching journey of his own. Following in his father’s footsteps, Jason graduated from Ganado and took his talents to the football field at Adams State University in Colorado. His journey through coaching led him to various institutions, including Adams State, the Colorado School of Mines, the University of Tulsa, Oklahoma State University, the University of Montana, Temple University, Valdosta State University, and Georgia Tech. Although he was not drafted into the NFL after college, he found inspiration in the playing styles of linebackers Dan Morgan from the Carolina Panthers and Hall of Famer Brian Urlacher of the Chicago Bears, a fellow New Mexican. “My father was my inspiration,” Jason confided. “He instilled in me the importance of teamwork and being part of something larger than myself. Growing up with him as a constant coach, I witnessed how investing in others and understanding team dynamics can lead to a shared joy in life."

As Semore settled into the coaching box, the weight of the game loomed large. "I went into the game feeling game ready," he chuckled, a hint of vulnerability in his voice. "But I would be lying if I told you that I wasn’t drinking Pepto Bismol up here because I had an ulcer." Despite his discomfort, the team rose to the occasion, executing plays with remarkable precision. Semore had also played a crucial role in promoting kicker Gavin Stewart to the spotlight. After Jude Kelley’s struggles in the first four games, Stewart stepped up, nailing all four of his field-goal attempts against Pitt. "Coach Key always says we’re crazy if we keep doing the same things and expect different results," Semore reflected. It was a pivotal moment, one where everyone felt a sense of comfort in making that decision. "Let’s give some opportunities to some different guys," he had thought, and one of those different guys was him—Jason Semore, a ball coach who was seizing his moment.

In the unfolding story of Tech's coaching staff, the focus today shifts to the new defensive coaches, as we delve into the second day of our exploration. At the heart of this narrative are two pivotal figures: David Turner, who wears multiple hats as the Assistant Head Coach for defense and Defensive Run Game Coordinator, and Jason Semore, the dedicated Linebackers Coach. Turner's title may be a mouthful, but his arrival is considered by many to be one of Tech's most significant hires in this cycle. Partnering with Brent Key, who also holds the title of Assistant Head Coach, Turner will collaborate closely with DB Coach Travares Tillman, the mastermind behind the team’s Defensive Pass Game strategy. Together, these coaches are set to shape the future of Tech's defense.

In 1986, Turner began his coaching journey at Davidson, where he took on the role of Running Backs and Tight Ends Coach. The following year, he shifted to the position of Linebackers Coach at the same institution. His path then led him to NC State from 1988 to 1989, where he served as a Graduate Assistant. By 1990, Turner was at the Indiana University of Pennsylvania, honing his skills as the Defensive Line Coach. He continued to build his expertise at James Madison from 1991 to 1992 as an Assistant Coach.

The years 1993 to 1994 found him at Kentucky, where he took charge as the Defensive Ends Coach. His time at NC State from 1995 to 1996 saw him working with Defensive Tackles and Linebackers, and soon after, he joined Virginia from 1997 to 2000, continuing his focus on Defensive Tackles and Linebackers. In 2001, he moved to Minnesota to coach the Defensive Ends before taking on the role of Defensive Line Coach and Recruiting Coordinator at Vanderbilt from 2002 to 2005.

His coaching journey didn't stop there; in 2006, he was back at Alabama as the Defensive Ends Coach, followed by a stint at Mississippi State from 2007 to 2009 as the Defensive Line Coach. He returned to Kentucky for a couple of years from 2010 to 2012, before making his way back to Mississippi State from 2013 to 2015, once again taking on the role of Defensive Line Coach. In the following years, Turner became the Run Defense Coordinator and Defensive Tackles Coach at Texas A&M from 2016 to 2017, and then moved to Texas-San Antonio as the Defensive Line Coach in 2018. His most recent positions included serving as the Defensive Line Coach at Florida from 2019 to 2021.

With such an extensive coaching background, Turner joined the staff, bringing with him a wealth of experience that made him one of the most seasoned coaches on the team.

Semore stood at the crossroads of his journey, fully aware that his upbringing was far from typical. With a father who coached, one might assume that the path to becoming a college coach was paved for him. Yet, he knew better. His childhood spent on a reservation and playing Division II football didn't offer the same connections that many of his peers enjoyed. However, those early years in Ganado, surrounded by the rich culture of the Navajo, imparted lessons that shaped him profoundly. It was there that he learned to navigate the diverse tapestry of human experience, forming bonds that came effortlessly to him. “Being able to establish relationships came naturally to me, and it was important to me, and it has allowed me to get to where I’m at today,” he reflected, a sense of pride evident in his voice. This journey was one he cherished deeply. As Saturday approached, anticipation filled the air. When Georgia Tech faced off against Pittsburgh, Semore, the coach’s son from Ganado, would step onto the field, ready to pen the next chapter of his remarkable career—an endeavor that few in college football had managed to replicate.

In the world of Wyoming football, Jovon Bouknight emerged as a Biletnikoff Award finalist, earning his place among the most prolific pass catchers in the program's storied history. With a wealth of experience and a skill set honed on the field, he transformed his passion for the game into a coaching career, beginning as a graduate assistant at his alma mater. His journey then led him to coaching positions at Utah State, Oregon, and Texas Tech, before he joined the coaching staff at Marshall after his time with the Kentucky Wildcats.

Meanwhile, Telly Lockette carved out his own path. A standout linebacker at Idaho State, he quickly made a name for himself as an innovative offensive mind in the high school football scene of Miami. His success there paved the way for his entry into the college ranks at South Florida. Lockette's journey continued as he took on roles at Oregon State, Florida State, and Miami (FL), ultimately becoming the running backs coach and run game coordinator at Marshall.

In the background, Tellek Lockette, Telly's son, was making waves as an emerging offensive lineman for Louisiana-Monroe in the Sun Belt, carrying on the family's football legacy. 

Shannon Morrison, a long-time fixture on the Marshall coaching staff, found himself in his third stint with the team, taking on the dual roles of linebacker coach and assistant head coach. His history with the program was rich; he had previously coached linebackers in Huntington from 2006 to 2006, and later focused on safeties from 2007 to 2008. As a former team captain and honorable All-American, he was proud to have been part of the 1992 FCS championship team.

Rounding out this narrative of dedication and achievement was Ralph Street, another notable figure in Thundering Herd history. As a team captain, he led his squad to victory, securing three MAC titles from 1998 to 2001, leaving an indelible mark on the program.

On a poignant December 20, friends, former teammates, and devoted fans gathered at McCamish Pavilion to celebrate the life of Thomas. It was a day filled with memories and reflections, a testament to the impact he had on those around him. Earlier, on April 27, 2022, Georgia Tech and the PeyBack Foundation—founded by Thomas's teammate Peyton Manning—announced the establishment of the Demaryius A. Thomas Scholarship Endowment. This endowment would provide academic scholarships for incoming freshmen from Laurens County, Georgia, Thomas's cherished hometown, ensuring his legacy would continue to support future generations.

In a fitting tribute, Georgia Tech designated August 8 as “Demaryius Thomas Day,” a day to honor his contributions both on and off the field. However, the season brought challenges, and on September 26, 2022, following the team's third loss in four games, Coach Geoff Collins and Athletics Director Todd Stansbury were relieved of their duties. In their place, former Georgia Tech football alum Brent Key stepped in as interim coach, ready to navigate the storm.

As anticipation grew, Georgia Tech unveiled its 2022 football schedule on February 1, 2022. The lineup featured five home games, six away contests, and a neutral-site matchup, highlighting the team's resilience. The Yellow Jackets would host ACC rivals Duke, Virginia, and Miami, while venturing to face formidable opponents such as Pitt, Florida State, Virginia Tech, and North Carolina. Their season culminated in a neutral-site showdown against Clemson at the iconic Mercedes-Benz Stadium in Atlanta, Georgia, where excitement and hope intertwined. Additionally, the Yellow Jackets welcomed two non-conference challengers, Western Carolina from Division I FCS and Ole Miss from the SEC, into their fold, setting the stage for a season filled with promise and determination.

The lapses during the season marked the end of coach Geoff Collins’ tenure, leading to losses that ultimately cost him his job and opened the door for Key to step in. Amidst the turmoil, Semore immersed himself in research, uncovering an alignment that positioned seven players on the line, with three robust linemen forming a protective barrier in front of Shanahan. “It was one of those moments where I thought, ‘What can I do about this? How can I prepare? How can I help?’” Semore reflected, his determination palpable. “I just want to see those guys have success.” 

As he shared his insights with Key, he sensed a shift in the atmosphere of the meeting. “Halfway through the conversation, I was like, ‘Am I being interviewed or something?’” Semore chuckled, recalling the unexpected turn. Once he finished outlining his ideas, Key surprised him with a proposition: “Well, would you be interested in being special-teams coordinator?” That was how the conversation unfolded, setting a new course for Semore.

Key’s role as interim coach was still fresh, just one game into his tenure. He was preparing to lead the Jackets against Duke at Bobby Dodd Stadium, stepping into the shoes of his alma mater’s team with a sense of purpose. The outcome of that lone game, a thrilling 26-21 road upset against then-No. 24 Pittsburgh, resonated deeply. Notably, the Jackets had faced an aggressive punt-block attack from Pitt but had allowed no blocked punts, affirming Key’s initial decision to place Semore at the helm of special teams.

Semore's journey through the coaching world had been a slow ascent since he graduated from Division II Adams State in 2005, where he once played linebacker. Each step along the way had taken him to four different Division II schools, the most recent being Valdosta State last year. Yet, with every stop, he garnered the respect of his peers. Kansas State cornerbacks coach Van Malone, a mentor to Semore, captured his essence perfectly when he remarked, “He is the essence of what coaching is.” Growing up in Ganado, Arizona, Semore felt the pull towards coaching, largely inspired by his father, Russ, a high school coach. “I never really had a choice,” he reflected, recognizing his calling to develop players and guide them toward their potential. “And how you do that is you educate your players on, ‘Listen, this is the process for having success,’ and it’s about educating them about the habits that are needed, being honest with them in the criticism aspect of it,” he explained. Now a family man, married with three children, Semore diligently maintains journals to track his progress. One is dedicated to X’s and O’s, while another focuses on player development and culture. He updates them daily, having filled roughly twelve throughout his career. “When I was growing up, my dad taught me that you’re ultimately going to be a product of two things,” he shared. “Your choices and the habits that you have."

Brent Key had just stepped into the role of Georgia Tech’s interim coach, a position that came with a pressing challenge. As he sought answers, he found himself in conversation with linebackers coach Jason Semore, intent on uncovering the secrets to preventing blocked punts. “Coach Key kind of asked me, ‘What do you see the issues are, how do you get this fixed?’” Semore recalled during a discussion with The Atlanta Journal-Constitution. The question weighed heavily on his mind, especially since the season opener when Clemson had thwarted two of their punts, leading to a disappointing 41-10 defeat at Mercedes-Benz Stadium. 

Determined to find a solution, Semore had been diligently researching various teams’ punt protection schemes, reaching out to colleagues, and compiling a video package that showcased around 160 punt plays. His goal wasn’t merely to identify the best scheme; he sought a straightforward yet effective protection plan—one that punter David Shanahan could grasp quickly, and which would still allow for a successful punt even in the event of a mistake. With the clock ticking, he pondered the most effective way to teach this new scheme over the course of a week, carefully mapping out the lessons day by day. As the Yellow Jackets stumbled through their initial games, with four punts blocked in just four outings, Semore found himself reflecting on these questions during many sleepless nights, driven by a desire to turn their fortunes around.

On an ordinary October day, Brent Key found himself abruptly summoned from an offensive game plan meeting. It was the fourth of the month when he was instructed to make the trek up the hill to the administrative offices of Georgia Tech. As he reached his destination, a wave of shock washed over him; head coach Geoff Collins had been let go. Key, a proud graduate of Georgia Tech who had once donned the uniform as a starting guard for all four years on The Flats, now faced a pivotal moment—he was to take the reins as head coach for the remainder of the season.

With a mix of determination and urgency, Key made his way back down the hill toward the football offices. At the age of 42, he was acutely aware of the immense responsibility that lay ahead of him. Before he could even entertain thoughts of leading Georgia Tech to victory against an FBS opponent—an elusive feat since their last triumph at Duke nearly a year prior—he knew he had to tackle an urgent issue that loomed large. Without hesitation, he headed directly to the office of linebackers coach Jason Semore, affectionately known as Boogie.

Semore could see it in Key’s eyes; they both understood the conversation that awaited them. The team’s punt protection had become a glaring problem that could no longer be ignored. In their first four games, the Yellow Jackets had suffered the indignity of having four punts blocked. Just two days earlier, a block against UCF had been returned for a touchdown, snatching away a lead that Georgia Tech had fought to establish just before halftime. The season opener had seen Clemson block two punts, while Ole Miss added to the woes with another block early in a brutal 42-0 defeat. Amidst the turmoil of a disheartening 1-3 start, it was clear to Key that rectifying the issues surrounding punt protection was his most pressing challenge.

In the vibrant world of college football, a trio of remarkable athletes stands out: Najee Harris, the dynamic running back from Alabama, Saquon Barkley, the talented product of Penn State, and Dominique Rodgers-Cromartie, a shining star from Tennessee State. Among those who have shaped the careers of such players is a man who also made his mark in the NFL, coaching with the Buffalo Bills in 2012 and working closely with running back C.J. Spiller. If you’ve tuned into the Netflix series Last Chance U, the name Clint Trickett might ring a bell. After hanging up his cleats, Trickett embarked on his coaching journey as the quarterbacks coach at East Mississippi Community College, a renowned powerhouse in junior college football.

As Trickett navigated his relatively young coaching career, success became a familiar companion. In 2021, he joined the Marshall coaching staff, stepping in as the wide receivers coach. His talent and dedication did not go unnoticed, and he quickly earned a promotion to offensive coordinator just in time for the 2022 college football season. In this pivotal role, he also took charge of the Thundering Herd’s quarterback room, where Cam Fancher stepped into the spotlight in 2023.

Before his arrival in Huntington, Trickett spent four years at Florida Atlantic, where he joined Lane Kiffin’s Owls staff in 2017 as the tight end coach. His influence was instrumental in the success of Harrison Bryant, who earned the prestigious John Mackey Award in 2019. This achievement paved the way for Trickett's ascent to co-offensive coordinator and quarterback coach following Kiffin's departure, solidifying his reputation in the world of football coaching.

It all started at Astronaut High School, where he embarked on his coaching journey, followed by a stint at the USAFA prep. From there, he carved out several years at the FCS level, lending his expertise to Alcorn State, Prairie View A&M, and Florida A&M. In 2021, he made his way to Marshall, stepping into the role of defensive line coach, a significant milestone in his career. query: When will Truth Social launch on iOS?
positive:
On a brisk February day in 2022, the digital landscape was forever altered with the release of Truth Social on Apple iOS. The app soared to the pinnacle of the App Store's charts, capturing the attention of many eager users. However, beneath the initial excitement lay a tangled web of complications. A staggering backlog of applicants greeted those who downloaded the app, and approximately 500,000 hopefuls found themselves automatically waitlisted, their ambitions momentarily halted.

In its first week, Truth Social boasted 872,000 installations, a promising start that soon dimmed. Just a month later, the influx of new signups dwindled to a mere 60,000 per week, while visits to truthsocial.com plummeted from 6 million to fewer than 2 million. Even as the app made headlines, it attracted scrutiny; the British automotive solar power company Trailar raised concerns, claiming that Truth Social's logo bore an uncanny resemblance to its own "T" emblem.

Critics were quick to voice their discontent with the app's performance at launch, highlighting the frustrations of waitlisted users and the ongoing outages that plagued the platform. A day after the much-anticipated launch, The Washington Post did not hold back, labeling the situation as "a disaster." A week later, Newsweek chronicled the waning enthusiasm of early adopters, some losing interest due to the dwindling user base and lackluster engagement, while others remained steadfast, hoping for improvement.

From the outset, Truth Social grappled with significant scalability issues, which hampered its potential for growth and left many wondering if the platform could ever overcome its rocky beginnings.

In the unfolding saga of social media, a new chapter began with the launch of Truth Social, a platform created by none other than Donald Trump. The anticipation was palpable, and on February 21, 2022—President’s Day in the United States—the app made its grand debut on iOS. As users flocked to the App Store, Truth Social surged to the top of the charts, a testament to the fervor surrounding its arrival.

The story behind Truth Social is steeped in controversy. Following Trump's bans from major platforms like Facebook and Twitter in 2021, which stemmed from concerns over his remarks after the Capitol Hill riots, the former president sought to carve out a space of his own. Once a titan of social media with millions of followers, Trump found himself ousted from the very platforms that had once amplified his voice. Undeterred, he set forth a bold plan to create an alternative, a refuge for his supporters and a new frontier in the digital landscape.

Truth Social is not just another app; it is the brainchild of Trump Media & Technology Group (TMTG), a company established in February 2021 with a clear mission: to offer alternatives to the mainstream media platforms that had turned their backs on him. As the narrative unfolds, Truth Social stands at the intersection of politics and technology, inviting users to explore what this new social network has to offer.

In a notable turn of events, Truth Social, a social media platform reminiscent of Twitter and linked to Donald Trump, has made its debut on the App Store, as reported by CNET. This announcement echoed a message from a verified account associated with "Billy B," the chief product officer of Truth Social, who urged users to circle February 21st on their calendars. "We’re currently set for release in the Apple App Store for Monday, February 21st," he shared, a sentiment that was later confirmed by Reuters. Coincidentally, this date aligns with Presidents' Day in the United States, adding a layer of significance to the occasion.

However, the launch is not without its apprehensions. query: When does the 2022 Olympic Winter Games end?

In the winter of 2022, the world turned its gaze to Beijing, where the XXIV Olympic Winter Games unfolded from February 4 to 20. Officially named the 2022 Winter Olympics, this grand event was a celebration of athleticism and international camaraderie, with competitions kicking off even earlier, on February 2. It marked the 24th edition of the Winter Olympic Games, a testament to the enduring spirit of sports. 

Beijing's journey to becoming the host city began in 2015 during the 128th IOC Session held in Kuala Lumpur, Malaysia. This momentous decision made Beijing the first city ever to host both the Summer and Winter Olympics, an honor that came after its successful hosting of the 2008 Summer Olympics. The 2022 Games were particularly significant, as they followed the 2018 Winter Olympics in Pyeongchang, South Korea, and the 2020 Summer Olympics in Tokyo, Japan, completing a remarkable trio of consecutive Olympic events hosted in East Asia. The city, alive with excitement and anticipation, was ready to welcome the world to a celebration of winter sports.

On the evening of February 20, 2022, the grand closing ceremony of the 2022 Winter Olympics unfolded at the iconic Beijing National Stadium. The atmosphere buzzed with excitement as a vibrant cultural presentation captivated the audience, followed by heartfelt closing remarks that marked the end of an extraordinary event. With a sense of anticipation, the formal handover took place, passing the torch to Milan and Cortina d'Ampezzo, the proud hosts of the upcoming 2026 Winter Olympics.

This edition of the Winter Games was remarkable, featuring a record-breaking 109 events spanning 15 disciplines across seven sports. Among the highlights were seven new medal events, showcasing the thrilling addition of men's and women's big air freestyle, women's monobob, and mixed team competitions in freestyle skiing aerials, ski jumping, and snowboard cross. The mixed relay in short track speed skating also made its debut, adding to the excitement.

As the ceremonies drew to a close, the numbers whispered tales of competition, with each discipline holding its own unique count of medal events. This journey had begun in October 2016 when the International Ski Federation (FIS) took a significant step, announcing plans to include women's competitions in Nordic combined. For the first time, Beijing would witness these athletes competing at the Olympic level. Then, in November 2017, the FIS proposed three additional events for Olympic consideration, including a ski jumping mixed team competition, along with men's and women's big air in freestyle skiing, further enriching the Olympic landscape.

As the final act of the Beijing 2022 Winter Olympics unfolded, a breathtaking firework display illuminated the night sky above the iconic Bird's Nest stadium. It was February 20, 2022, a chilly evening that marked the conclusion of the Games, which had been shadowed by the specter of COVID-19, political tensions, and the controversy of a doping scandal that involved Russia. For 16 days, athletes from around the world had competed in 109 events, and now, the traditional closing ceremony signaled an end to the extraordinary chapter.

A small but spirited crowd filled the stands, their breath visible in the frigid air as they awaited the festivities. Some waved the Chinese flag enthusiastically, while others joined in a cheerful wave that swept through the audience. Among them, Chinese President Xi Jinping and International Olympic Committee President Thomas Bach shared a moment, observing the ceremony together.

The performances during the closing ceremony were intentionally restrained, mirroring the understated elegance of the opening ceremony that had taken place earlier in the month. The theme remained consistent, celebrating the beauty of snow and ice, as the world bid farewell to an Olympic experience that was anything but ordinary.

As the sun began to set on February 20, 2022, members of Team Denmark made their grand entrance into the iconic Beijing National Stadium. It was Day 16 of the Beijing 2022 Winter Olympics, and the atmosphere was electric as anticipation filled the air during the Closing Ceremony. 

Amidst the celebrations, the spotlight turned to the medalists of the Men's Cross-Country Skiing 50km Mass Start Free. In the center stood the gold medallist, Alexander Bolshunov of Team ROC, proudly displaying his medal. To his left, silver medallist Ivan Yakimushkin, also from Team ROC, beamed with joy, while to the right, bronze medallist Simen Hegstad Krueger of Team Norway shared in the moment of triumph.

As performers danced gracefully, the Olympic Cauldron flickered in the background, casting a warm glow over the festivities. The scene inside the Beijing National Stadium was a tapestry of color and emotion, encapsulating the spirit of the Games on this unforgettable evening.

As the snow season swept in, lasting five months from November, Chongli transformed into a vibrant hub of winter sports, hosting an impressive thirty-six competitions and activities, including the prestigious Far East Cup and the enchanting Children Skiing International Festival. The community came alive with the establishment of twenty-three skiing camps, drawing in around 3,800 enthusiastic youths eager to embrace the slopes. The groundwork for the venues began in November 2016, and by the end of 2020, they stood ready, paving the way for test events that would showcase the region's capabilities.

On a chilly October day in 2021, the design for the Games' medals was unveiled, a stunning tribute inspired by the medals of the 2008 Summer Olympics, intertwined with elements of Chinese astronomy and astrology, perfectly timed with the joyous celebrations of the Chinese New Year. The anticipation continued to build as the uniforms for the medal presenters were revealed in January 2022, a creative collaboration between the Central Academy of Fine Arts and the Beijing Institute of Fashion Technology.

The journey of the Olympic torch began its historic route on October 18, 2021, in Greece, igniting the spirit of the Games. With excitement, it was announced that the local leg of the relay would commence on February 2 and conclude on February 4, 2022, coinciding with the grand Opening Ceremonies, though it would only grace the cities of Beijing and Zhangjiakou. Amidst the celebration, a group of activists staged a protest at the Olympic torch lighting ceremony in Greece, adding a poignant reminder of the complexities surrounding such a monumental event.

In 2019, the completion of the Beijing-Zhangjiakou high-speed railway transformed travel between these two destinations, allowing passengers to journey from Beijing to Yanqing in a mere 20 minutes, and to Zhangjiakou in just 50 minutes. This advancement set the stage for the Olympic Winter Games in 2022, an event that would showcase not only athletic prowess but also a commitment to gender equality. With the introduction of seven new events—women’s monobob, freestyle skiing big air for both men and women, and mixed team events in short track speed skating team relay, ski jumping, freestyle skiing aerials, and snowboard cross—the Games became the most gender-balanced in history, featuring 45.44 percent female athletes and more women’s events than ever before. As February 2022 arrived, so too did the excitement of the Olympic Winter Games, coinciding with the cherished Chinese New Year, a time of celebration and cultural significance. Local residents opened their arms to athletes from around the world, inviting them to partake in the vibrant festivities, admire the breathtaking landscapes, and immerse themselves in the rich traditions of Beijing, Yanqing, and Zhangjiakou. The spirit of the Games was alive, with teams competing fiercely and medals awaited by all.

On the evening of February 20, 2022, the Beijing National Stadium buzzed with excitement as performers took to the stage during the Closing Ceremony of the Beijing 2022 Winter Olympics. It was Day 16 of the grand event, and the atmosphere was electric. Among the highlights, children from Milan and Cortina held a globe, symbolizing the handover ceremony, their faces shining with joy and anticipation. The Olympic Cauldron flickered in the background, a beacon of the spirit of competition and unity that had defined the Games. Amidst the celebration, it was noted that a previous report had mistakenly claimed the U.S. finished in third place for the total medal count; in reality, they secured fifth place. Brian Mann contributed to this unfolding story, capturing the essence of a moment that marked the end of an extraordinary chapter in Olympic history.

As the sun set on that memorable Sunday, the atmosphere was charged with anticipation during the ceremony. IOC leader Bach stepped forward, the Olympic flag in hand, ready to pass this cherished symbol to the mayors of Milan and Cortina, the proud hosts of the upcoming 2026 Winter Games. Meanwhile, on a towering screen, the image of Xi Jinping, the President of China, flickered to life, coinciding with the graceful ascent of the Olympic flag prior to the grand Closing Ceremony of the Beijing 2022 Winter Olympics. The International Olympic Committee had made a bold demand, seeking control over all advertising space in Oslo, intending it to be reserved solely for its sponsors. However, this was a tall order in Norway, where the government did not own or wield control over all advertising within the city and lacked the authority to grant exclusive rights to a foreign private entity over urban spaces and private properties. Many observers were astonished by such audacity, noting that it seemed out of place in a western democracy. Slate went as far as to label the IOC a "notoriously ridiculous organization run by grifters and hereditary aristocrats." Ole Berget, the deputy finance minister, echoed the sentiments of the public, remarking that the IOC's arrogance had become a rallying point for many. The largest newspaper in the country weighed in, stating that while Norway was indeed wealthy, its citizens were reluctant to spend money on what they deemed inappropriate demands from IOC officials. "These outrageous requests, expecting to be treated like royalty, simply wouldn’t resonate with the Norwegian public," they declared. 

As the dust settled on the controversy, a decision was made regarding the host city for the 2022 Winter Olympics. On July 31, 2015, during the 128th IOC Session in Kuala Lumpur, Malaysia, Beijing emerged victorious over Almaty by a narrow margin of four votes. In February 2021, the city made a significant announcement, revealing that all 26 venues, including those for training, would operate entirely on renewable energy.

In a world where tensions brewed beneath the surface, whispers of conspiracy echoed through the halls of power. The United States and its allies were said to be crafting schemes aimed at undermining China's efforts to host the Olympics, a move that many viewed as a grave affront to the very essence of the Olympic Charter. This was not merely a political maneuver; it threatened to tarnish China's image on the international stage. In response to these perceived slights, a firm rejection of such actions was declared.

As the Olympic Games approached, excitement built among athletes and nations alike. A total of 91 National Olympic Committees had secured spots for their competitors, two fewer than in the previous edition. The grand competition began two days ahead of the glitzy opening ceremony on February 2, 2022, and extended until February 20. Organizers faced the daunting task of revising schedules, with each iteration requiring the nod of approval from the International Olympic Committee. When the dust settled and the final medals were awarded, Norway emerged triumphant at the top of the medal table for the second Winter Olympics in a row. They amassed a staggering 37 medals, 16 of which were gold, setting a new record for the most golds won in a single Winter Olympics.

Germany followed closely behind, claiming 12 golds and a total of 27 medals. The host nation, China, proudly stood in third place, securing nine gold medals—marking their most successful Winter Olympics to date. On January 21, 2022, a significant moment in history unfolded as Ford Greene, Ralph Long Jr., and Lawrence Williams, Georgia Tech’s first Black students, along with Ronald Yancey, the institution’s first Black graduate, were honored with the 2022 Ivan Allen Jr. Prize for Social Courage. This prestigious award, established in 2010 to commemorate the legacy of Tech alumnus and former Atlanta Mayor Ivan Allen Jr., is funded in perpetuity by a generous grant from the Wilbur and Hilda Glenn Family Foundation and carries a stipend of $100,000 for its recipients. The very first award was presented in March 2011, recognizing those courageous leaders who, much like Mayor Allen himself, have faced personal risks in their unwavering commitment to enhancing the lives of others. Georgia Tech President Ángel Cabrera spoke passionately about the recipients: “With great determination, Ford Greene, Ralph Long Jr., Lawrence Williams, and Ronald Yancey withstood hazards and adversity of every kind to prove to the world that Black students had the right to study at Georgia Tech, paving the way for the thousands of Black Yellow Jackets who have earned their degrees here since.” 

These three pioneers, immortalized in a sculpture at Harrison Square that captures their first day at Tech, embarked on their academic journey in the fall of 1961. Though their arrival did not provoke the violence witnessed at other southern universities, their path was fraught with challenges.

In a moment that blended history with celebration, the House of Representatives’ John Lewis and former U.S. President and Tech alumnus Jimmy Carter were among the notable figures who inspired the community. It was a decision that might have seemed unconventional for the nomination committee and the Tech President to confer an award upon a student of the Institute, yet it resonated profoundly as it marked a significant milestone: 60 years of Black student matriculation at Tech. The Institute had come to understand that its diversity was not just a benefit but its very strength. Reflecting on the past, the names of Ford Greene, Ralph Long Jr., the late Lawrence Williams, and Ronald Yancey emerged, each having made remarkable strides during their time at Tech. Their social courage had left an indelible mark, shaping the campus atmosphere that thrived to this day. Thanks to their contributions, generations of Black Yellow Jackets had forged a path forward. The community would gather on April 20, 2022, from 10 a.m. to 2 p.m. in the Biltmore Ballroom at Tech Square, ready to honor these four pioneering figures and celebrate their enduring legacy.

In the autumn of 1961, a significant chapter unfolded at Georgia Tech as Ford Greene, Ralph Long Jr., and Lawrence Williams stepped onto campus as the institution's first Black students. Their journey was not merely a personal milestone; it was a bold statement against the prevailing norms of the time. Alongside them was Ronald Yancey, who would later make history as Tech’s first Black graduate. Decades after they shattered the color barrier, these four men are poised to receive the esteemed 2022 Ivan Allen Jr. Prize for Social Courage, joining the ranks of illustrious past honorees like Jimmy and Rosalynn Carter, John Lewis, and Andrew Young.

The likenesses of Greene, Long, Williams, and Yancey now stand immortalized in campus sculptures, testament to their courage and resilience. Georgia Tech President Ángel Cabrera remarked on their legacy, stating, “The Ivan Allen Jr. Prize recognizes exemplary, courageous leaders — those who, like Mayor Allen, take on personal risks in an effort to improve the lives of others.” He emphasized the determination these pioneers displayed, facing hazards and adversity to assert their rightful place in academia, thereby paving the way for countless Black Yellow Jackets who followed in their footsteps.

As the “three pioneers” are immortalized in the Harrison Square sculpture—capturing their first day at Tech—their arrival, though not met with violence as it had been at other southern universities, was fraught with challenges. Their story is one of bravery and perseverance, a narrative woven into the very fabric of Georgia Tech’s history.

In the year 1961, a momentous chapter unfolded at Georgia Tech when three courageous Black students—Lawrence Williams, Ralph Long Jr., and the late Ford Greene—stepped onto campus, marking a historic milestone as the first institution in the Deep South to integrate peacefully without a court order. At that time, civil unrest simmered across college campuses throughout the South, yet these trailblazers forged ahead, undeterred by the turbulent climate. 

Fast forward to April 20, 2022, in the elegant Biltmore Ballroom in Atlanta, where their legacy was honored with the prestigious Ivan Allen Jr. Prize for Social Courage. Alongside them was Ronald Yancey, the first Black graduate of Georgia Tech. The atmosphere buzzed with anticipation as the panel was led by 2020 Ivan Allen Jr. Prize recipient Charlayne Hunter-Gault. Long Jr., Williams, and Yancey shared their experiences of integration at the Institute, painting vivid pictures of their struggles against racism, which lurked beneath the surface of the ostensibly peaceful transition. 

Despite feeling isolated from the majority of their peers, these pioneers held onto a flicker of hope, envisioning a future in which Black students at Tech would feel welcomed and at home. “I’m so thankful for those who continue to matriculate here and carry on the tradition,” Williams expressed, his voice filled with gratitude. It was on a significant day, September 27, 1961, that three courageous black students took their first steps into the classrooms of the Institute, marking a historic moment as Tech became the first higher education institution to integrate in the Deep South. This act of peaceful integration, accomplished without a court order, was a bold move during a time when such endeavors were often met with hostility and violence, sparking civil unrest across college campuses throughout the Southern states.

This prestigious award, established in 2010 to celebrate the legacy of beloved Tech alumnus and former Atlanta mayor Ivan Allen Jr., embodies the spirit of service and progress that he championed. On a chilly day, the 13th of February 2022, the National Speed Skating Oval, known as the "Ice Ribbon," in Beijing welcomed an electrifying atmosphere as the women’s 500 m competition unfolded during the Winter Olympics. In a stunning display of speed and skill, Erin Jackson from the United States claimed the title of Olympic champion, marking her first-ever Olympic medal and making history as the first female Black athlete to stand on the podium in speed skating. 

As the race progressed, Miho Takagi of Japan skated fiercely to secure the silver medal, while Angelina Golikova, representing the Russian Olympic Committee, captured bronze, also her first Olympic medal. Among the competitors was Nao Kodaira, the defending champion and Olympic record holder, though the spotlight was stolen by the newcomers. The 2018 silver medalist, Lee Sang-hwa, had stepped away from competition, yet she still held the world record during these games. Another retired athlete, Karolína Erbanová, who had once claimed bronze, watched from the sidelines.

In the backdrop of this competition, Golikova had already made a name for herself as the 2021 World Single Distances champion in the 500 m event. The stakes were high, with Erin Jackson leading the 2021–22 ISU Speed Skating World Cup at the 500 m distance, having completed eight events before the Olympics. Following her were Golikova and Kodaira, each vying for glory. Golikova had impressed the crowd earlier that season, skating a season best time of 36.66 in Calgary on December 11, 2021. As the competition unfolded, Takagi in pair 4 took an early lead, clocking in at 37.12, setting the stage for an unforgettable race filled with determination and triumph.

Alongside her teammates, Brittany Bowe and Kimi Goetz, she was set to race in the women's 500m event. Just four years prior, Jackson had been a newcomer to the sport, earning a place on the U.S. Olympic team in 2018. Now, with a series of victories under her belt, she emerged as a fierce contender for a medal. “I perform better under pressure,” she reflected, her eyes shining with determination. “When it's a high-pressure situation like Olympic Trials or the Olympics, I'm kind of feeding off of that energy. I just feel ready.” 

Bowe, who had claimed victory in the 500m at the U.S. Trials, primarily excelled in the 1000m and 1500m distances. During her trial run, however, Jackson had stumbled, finishing third and missing an automatic qualification. In a surprising and generous twist, Bowe chose to forfeit her spot, allowing Jackson the chance to compete. Fortunately for Bowe, the U.S. team secured a third qualifier, granting her the opportunity to skate as well. 

It had been years since an American woman had clinched the gold in the 500m; the last to do so was Bonnie Blair, who achieved a remarkable third consecutive victory at the 1994 Olympics. Competing against them was Nao Kodaira of Japan, the defending gold medalist, who had made history in 2018 by setting the Olympic record and becoming the first Japanese skater to win the women's 500m event. As she entered her fourth Olympic Games at the age of 35, she stood in third place in the World Cup standings, ready to defend her title.

The women's 500m race was set to kick off on a brisk Sunday at 8:56 a.m. ET, with fans glued to their screens to catch the excitement live on NBC.

Among the athletes was Erin Jackson, poised to make her Winter Olympic debut alongside teammates Brittany Bowe and Kimi Goetz. The journey to bring "The Endgame" to life began on April 21, 2021, when NBC greenlit what was then known as The Untitled Nick Wootton/Jake Coburn Project. Despite instances where the pacing may falter, the series unflinchingly delves into the complexities of the criminal mind, provoking thought and evoking unease. Among the pantheon of infamous fictional killers, Hannibal Lecter reigns supreme, a character who has haunted the pages of literature and the screens of cinema alike, notably in the eponymous television series. What sets Hannibal apart from the typical fare on network television is its audacious stylization of violence and the palpable chemistry that crackles between Hannibal and Will Graham. Venturing into the realm of criminal investigations, The Endgame further illuminates the inner workings of the FBI, captivating those eager to understand the intricate dance of law and order. In the heart of the story lies a compelling pattern, as Assane, a man of remarkable skills and undeniable charisma, plots a meticulously crafted robbery aimed at a man who had wronged his father a quarter of a century ago.
