query: When did Russia invade Ukraine?
positive: In a dramatic turn of events, he asserted, albeit falsely, that they had been enduring "humiliation and genocide at the hands of the Kyiv regime." Putin went on to make more incendiary claims, declaring that the Ukrainian government was infested with neo-Nazis under Western influence, that Ukraine was secretly developing nuclear weapons, and that NATO was amassing military resources within Ukraine to pose a threat to Russia. He proclaimed that Russia aimed for the "demilitarisation and denazification" of Ukraine, while insisting he had no intention of occupying the country and advocated for the Ukrainian people's right to self-determination. However, mere moments after his announcement, the sky lit up with Russian missiles striking targets across Ukraine, as troops surged in from the north, east, and south. Not long after, an alleged document from Russia's Federal Security Service (FSB) surfaced, revealing that the agency had been blindsided by Putin's invasion plan. The dawn of 24 February marked the beginning of the invasion, as infantry divisions, backed by armored units and air support, swept into Eastern Ukraine, unleashing a barrage of missile attacks that reached as far west as Lviv. The first clashes erupted in Luhansk Oblast, near the village of Milove on the Russian border, at precisely 3:40 a.m. Kyiv time.

On February 24, 2022, the world was taken aback when Russia launched an invasion of Ukraine, igniting a fierce conflict that continues to unfold. At the heart of this turmoil was President Vladimir Putin, who offered a series of shifting justifications for his country's aggressive actions. As the narrative of this invasion unfolded, it became essential to explore not only the reasons Putin publicly shared but also the deeper realities at play and the potential motivations behind Russia's military incursion into a sovereign nation.

Ukraine's history is intertwined with Russia's, having been part of the Soviet Union until it boldly declared independence in 1991, solidified by a referendum just days before the USSR's dissolution. Since that pivotal moment, Ukraine has fiercely upheld its sovereignty. Yet, Putin has consistently portrayed Ukraine as an extension of Russia, dismissing its right to exist as an independent entity. In a notable exchange with then-US President George W. Bush in 2008, he asserted that Ukraine wasn’t even a country, a sentiment echoed by many Russians, as noted by Stephen Hall, a scholar of Russian politics at the University of Bath in the UK. 

Hall explained that this belief is not confined to the Kremlin; it permeates a significant portion of Russian society. To many, Kyiv, the capital of Ukraine, is seen as the "mother of Russian cities," a significant historical touchstone that Putin cannot bear to see outside of Russian influence. Hall further elaborated that for Russia to maintain its narrative as a great power with a legacy spanning millennia, it must assert its claim over Ukraine. Without the historical significance of Kyiv, he argued, Russia would struggle to validate its own past, as Kyiv had already flourished 1,200 years ago when Moscow was merely a forest.

On the fateful day of February 24, 2022, the world watched as Russia launched an invasion of Ukraine, marking a dramatic escalation in the ongoing Russo-Ukrainian War that had begun back in 2014. The consequences of this aggressive act were devastating, claiming the lives of tens of thousands on both sides and leaving a trail of destruction in its wake. As Russian forces advanced, they became notorious for their brutal tactics, inflicting mass civilian casualties and subjecting captured Ukrainian soldiers to horrific torture. 

By June 2022, the impact of the war was felt deeply within Ukraine, with approximately 8 million people forced to flee their homes, seeking safety within their own country. The crisis soon escalated further, as more than 8.2 million Ukrainians sought refuge beyond their borders by May 2023, leading to the largest refugee crisis Europe had seen since World War II. Meanwhile, the conflict wrought extensive environmental damage, a devastation that many described as ecocide, triggering food crises that echoed around the globe.

In the lead-up to the invasion, Russian troops had been amassing near Ukraine's borders, while Russian officials adamantly denied any intentions of launching an attack. It was against this backdrop that Vladimir Putin declared a "special military operation," ostensibly to support the breakaway republics of Donetsk and Luhansk, whose military forces were already entrenched in conflict with Ukraine in the Donbas region. He claimed that the operation aimed to "demilitarise" and "denazify" Ukraine, all while espousing irredentist views that questioned Ukraine's very right to exist. In a narrative steeped in falsehoods, he painted a picture of a nation governed by neo-Nazis who were persecuting the ethnic Russian minority, setting the stage for a conflict that would reshape the lives of millions.

As the sun rose on February 24, 2022, Vladimir Putin made a fateful decision, sending up to 200,000 soldiers into Ukraine with the belief that he could swiftly seize the capital, Kyiv, and overthrow its government within days. However, the reality soon proved to be far different; his ambitious invasion plan unraveled amid a series of humiliating retreats. While the initial strategy had clearly failed, the conflict was far from over. Even now, Putin referred to this monumental European invasion—the largest since World War Two—as merely a "special military operation," refusing to acknowledge the full-scale war that had rained bombs upon civilians and forced more than 13 million people to flee their homes, either seeking refuge abroad or becoming internally displaced.

On that fateful day, Putin declared his intent to "demilitarise and denazify" Ukraine, insisting that he did not aim to occupy the nation by force. This proclamation came just days after he had endorsed the independence of eastern Ukrainian territories that had fallen under the sway of Russian proxy forces since 2014. He painted a picture of a protector, claiming to shield the people from eight years of alleged Ukrainian bullying and genocide—a narrative wholly lacking in truth. His rhetoric included a desire to prevent NATO from establishing a presence in Ukraine, coupled with the aim of ensuring the nation’s neutral status. Yet, unspoken but looming in the shadows of his ambitions was the goal of toppling the democratically elected president of Ukraine. Volodymyr Zelensky, aware of the peril he faced, remarked, "The enemy has designated me as target number one; my family is target number two," capturing the gravity of the situation that had unfolded.

As the air-raid siren blared through the streets of Kyiv on February 26, 2022, people scrambled for shelter near an apartment building scarred by recent shelling. Just two days earlier, on February 24, Russia had launched a large-scale invasion of Ukraine, igniting a conflict that stemmed from decades of tension between the two nations. The roots of this fraught relationship stretched back to 1991, when Ukraine, after the fall of the Soviet Union, boldly declared its independence from Moscow. 

In 2004, the air was thick with political strife as pro-Russian candidate Viktor Yanukovich was declared president amid allegations of vote-rigging. This triggered widespread protests, known as the Orange Revolution, culminating in a re-run of the election that saw the pro-Western Viktor Yushchenko ascend to the presidency in 2005. Yushchenko promised to steer Ukraine away from the Kremlin's influence, aiming for closer ties with NATO and the European Union. 

By 2008, NATO had extended a promise to Ukraine, assuring its future membership in the alliance. However, the winds of change shifted in 2010 when Yanukovich reclaimed the presidency. In 2013, tensions escalated as Yanukovich's government abruptly halted trade and association talks with the EU, opting instead to rekindle economic relations with Moscow. This decision ignited months of mass rallies in the heart of Kyiv, culminating in February 2014 when Parliament, responding to the bloodshed of the protests, voted to remove Yanukovich from power. Just days later, armed men stormed the parliament in Crimea, raising the Russian flag and marking a dramatic turning point in the region's history.

In the midst of a tumultuous year, the war in Ukraine unfolded, claiming thousands of lives and displacing millions from their homes. Entire cities crumbled under the weight of conflict, and a palpable tension loomed as the world feared an escalation into a broader confrontation involving Russia and NATO. On February 24, the narrative shifted dramatically when Russian President Vladimir Putin ordered an invasion of Ukraine, advancing from the north, east, and south. He referred to this as a “special military operation,” justifying it with claims of aiming for the “demilitarization” and “denazification” of Ukraine, all in the name of protecting ethnic Russians and halting Kyiv's NATO ambitions. However, Ukraine and its Western allies denounced this as an illegal act of aggression against a nation with a democratically elected government, helmed by a Jewish president whose family had suffered during the Holocaust. As Russian troops swiftly approached Kyiv’s outskirts, they encountered fierce resistance in their bid to seize the capital and other cities in the northeast. In a bold move to rally his nation, Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelenskyy recorded a video outside his headquarters, determined to demonstrate that he remained steadfast and in command amidst the chaos.

On a fateful day, June 10, 2022, Vadym Skibitsky, the deputy head of Ukraine's military intelligence, stood amidst the turmoil of the Severodonetsk campaign. With a heavy heart, he remarked on the crucial importance of the frontlines, declaring, "This is an artillery war now, and we are losing in terms of artillery. Everything now depends on what [the west] gives us." His words painted a stark picture: for every single artillery piece Ukraine had, there were ten to fifteen on the Russian side. He revealed that the support from Western partners amounted to a mere ten percent of what was needed.

As the days passed, the situation grew ever more dire. On June 29, Reuters reported on Director of National Intelligence Avril Haines, who delivered a sobering update on U.S. intelligence assessments regarding the Russian invasion. She conveyed that the consensus among U.S. intelligence agencies was that the invasion would persist "for an extended period of time." The outlook, she stated, was grim, and Russia’s stance towards the West was hardening.

By July 5, the BBC reported on the devastating impact of the invasion, highlighting the extensive destruction it wrought on Ukraine’s economy. Ukrainian Prime Minister Denys Shmyhal, addressing a reconstruction conference in Switzerland, made a poignant plea: "Ukraine needs $750bn for a recovery plan, and Russian oligarchs should contribute to the cost."

The invasion had begun earlier, on February 24, when forces launched from Belarus with the intent to seize Kyiv. Simultaneously, an assault unfolded from the northeast against Kharkiv, while the southeastern front advanced through two separate spearheads—one from Crimea and another from the southeast, targeting Luhansk and Donetsk. The landscape of Ukraine was forever altered by the violence that unfolded.

NATO had long maintained that it was not in the business of seeking confrontation, a stance that persisted even during periods of cooperation with Russia. However, the landscape shifted dramatically following Russia's annexation of Crimea. In February 2022, as tensions escalated, French President Emmanuel Macron and German Chancellor Olaf Scholz took it upon themselves to try to avert war. Macron, in a bid for peace, met with Putin, but his persuasive efforts fell flat, and the Russian leader remained resolute in his intentions. Scholz, too, attempted to sway Putin, warning of severe sanctions should he choose to invade Ukraine. In a last-ditch effort to negotiate, Scholz advised Ukrainian President Zelenskyy to declare neutrality and abandon any dreams of NATO membership. Yet, Zelenskyy was not convinced; he believed that trusting Putin to honor such a settlement would be folly.

As the days progressed, on February 21, Putin addressed the nation, declaring that Russia would recognize the territories in Ukraine under its control—the Donetsk People's Republic and Luhansk People's Republic—as independent states. The very next day, he announced the deployment of troops into these regions, branding them "peacekeepers." In a significant move, the Federation Council of Russia granted authorization for military force abroad. Then, on the early morning of February 24, just before 5 a.m. in Kyiv, Putin made yet another address to the world. He proclaimed the launch of a "special military operation," an announcement that Business Insider later characterized as tantamount to declaring war on Ukraine. In his speech, he emphasized that the operation aimed to "protect the people" of the Donbas, situated in the breakaway republics of Donetsk and Luhansk, now under Russian control.

It was on the fateful day of 24 February 2022 that Russia launched its invasion of Ukraine, a significant escalation in a conflict that had its roots in the Russo-Ukrainian War, which had begun back in 2014. This aggressive move resulted in the tragic loss of tens of thousands of lives...

Three days ago, on February 24, 2022, a tense stillness hung in the air as the world turned its gaze to a moment that would change history. Russia, with its mighty forces, crossed the border into Ukraine, igniting a conflict that would resonate far beyond their shared frontier. The story of these two nations, once intertwined by history and culture, took a dramatic turn that day, beckoning the world to witness the unfolding tale.

As this intricate web of support and sanction spun tighter, reports emerged from the United States indicating that Chinese ammunition had made its way to battlefields in Ukraine. By May 2023, the European Union had traced the supply of weapon components from Chinese and UAE firms to Russia, highlighting the international stakes at play. Then, in June 2023, U.S. military intelligence suggested that Iran had also contributed, providing materials for UAV production to the Russians, further entangling the players in this global conflict.

On January 29, 2022, a question lingered in the air: Why did the West seek to expand NATO, and how did Russia respond to this ambition? The roots of this inquiry stretched back to 1991, the year the Soviet Union crumbled, marking the beginning of a complex dialogue about NATO's expansion.

On February 16, 2022, the civil war unfolded like a dark cloud over Eastern Europe, but for Putin, it was a strategic advantage. It provided Moscow with a means to exert pressure, a tool to compel Ukraine into submission whenever the Kremlin deemed it necessary.

In the early days of 2014, a storm began to brew in eastern Ukraine, ignited by Russia's bold annexation of Crimea. Just a month later, in March, the situation escalated as Russian troops moved in, seizing control of...

On a fateful day in February 2022, the world watched as Russia initiated a full-scale invasion of Ukraine, a move that would send shockwaves across the globe. As the dust began to settle, the motivations behind this aggressive act became a topic of discussion among experts. In the summer of 2023, Insider sought the insights of three knowledgeable voices, each offering their perspectives on the complex reasons that led to this unprecedented conflict.

query: When was Elden Ring being released?
positive: In a time long past, there existed a radiant light, a golden aura that shone brightly in the eyes of a chosen few. This illumination, a symbol of the blessing and grace bestowed by the mighty Erdtree, set them apart from others. Yet, as the years unfolded, some individuals found themselves stripped of this divine favor, their once-glimmering eyes now dulled and devoid of that precious light. These forsaken souls became known as the Tarnished, cast away and exiled from their world long before the player steps into this tale. With the passage of time, the fabled Elden Ring lay shattered, and now, the Tarnished are called back to The Lands Between, beckoned by fate itself. So, what are you waiting for? Secure your pre-order, and prepare to embark on this epic journey. Elden Ring will make its grand debut on February 25, 2022, available on PS4, PS5, Xbox One, Xbox Series X, and PC. Stay tuned for more updates. This is the voice of music and pop culture, breaking the news of what’s new and what’s next since 1952. When you purchase through links on our site, we may earn an affiliate commission. © 2023 NME is part of NME Networks.

In a realm known as The Lands Between, where danger lurks around every corner and monstrous beasts lie in wait, a call to adventure beckons those who dare to embrace the challenge. If you consider yourself a glutton for punishment and are prepared to endure the trials that await, join us as we embark on a journey to the heartlands, where survival in this grimdark fantasy epic is not just an option, but a necessity. 

The tale unfolds with the release of Elden Ring on February 25, 2022, ready to captivate players on PS4, PS5, Xbox One, Xbox Series X, and PC. For those brave enough to pre-order on Xbox One or PS4, a free next-gen upgrade awaits, a small token for the intrepid. The quest for this adventure begins at a price of £59.99 in the UK and $59.99 in the US, but for collectors seeking something truly special, the journey doesn't stop there.

Among the treasures lies the collector’s edition, priced at £189.99, which offers not only the game but also a striking steelbook, a 40-page art book filled with visions of the world, a digital soundtrack to accompany your battles, and a 9-inch statue of Malenia – Blade of Miquella, a figure of both beauty and terror. For those willing to pay a premium, the premium collector's edition stands at an astonishing £259.99, including all previous items along with an official helmet replica of Malenia, a testament to her formidable legacy.

Adventurers can secure their editions, including the standard, through Bandi Namco’s official website, where the journey begins. As chaos reigns in the realm, the shattered Elden Ring, the very source of the Erdtree, sets the stage for an epic saga filled with peril and discovery.

In the bustling corridors of Bandai Namco, the excitement was palpable as November 2021 marked a significant milestone—the launch of a closed beta for a game that had been shrouded in anticipation. Players eagerly signed up, eager to delve into the realms of Elden Ring. Originally slated for release on January 21, 2022, the game's full unveiling was pushed back to February 25, creating a wave of speculation and impatience among fans.

When the day finally arrived, however, not everything unfolded smoothly. Gamers quickly voiced their frustrations as performance issues marred the experience, with many lamenting the game's insufficient frame rate. In response, Bandai Namco sprang into action, rolling out software patches and updates to address the concerns of their players.

As the dust settled from the initial launch, the creative minds at Steamforged Games launched a crowdfunding campaign on Kickstarter in November 2022, seeking to bring an Elden Ring-themed board game to life. Then, in February 2023, the excitement reignited with the announcement of a forthcoming expansion titled Shadow of the Erdtree, promising to enrich the Elden Ring experience even further.

Despite the rocky start, Elden Ring captured the hearts of many, earning "universal acclaim" on the review aggregator Metacritic. The game was a sensation on the streaming platform Twitch, attracting nearly 900,000 viewers within the first 24 hours, marking it as the third-most-popular debut, trailing only behind Lost Ark and Cyberpunk 2077.

Reviewers were particularly taken with the game's expansive open-world setting, praising its exploration mechanics. Tamoor Hussain from GameSpot heralded the Lands Between as the most vast of FromSoftware's creations, emphasizing that the thrill of exploration and discovery was what made the game truly shine. Similarly, Mitchell Saltzman from IGN celebrated Elden Ring for its ability to reward players who ventured into every corner of the richly crafted map.

In the year 2022, a remarkable journey began with the release of "Elden Ring," an action role-playing game crafted by the talented team at FromSoftware. At the helm was Hidetaka Miyazaki, a visionary director, whose ambition was further fueled by the imaginative insights of fantasy author George R. R. Martin, who lent his worldbuilding expertise to the project. The game made its debut on February 25, reaching eager players on platforms such as PlayStation 4, PlayStation 5, Windows, Xbox One, and Xbox Series X/S, first in Japan and then to the rest of the world through Bandai Namco Entertainment.

As players stepped into this vast realm, they took on the role of a customizable hero, embarking on a noble quest to restore the shattered Elden Ring and ascend as the new Elden Lord. The adventure unfolded from a third-person perspective, allowing explorers to roam freely across a richly interactive open world. To traverse the diverse landscapes, they relied on their faithful steed, Torrent, while also discovering hidden dungeons that held valuable treasures.

Armed with an array of weapons and powerful magic spells, players could choose their approach to combat, embracing stealth to outmaneuver foes. Throughout their travels, they encountered checkpoints that facilitated swift travel and offered opportunities to enhance their abilities using Runes, a precious in-game currency. The game also featured an online multiplayer mode, inviting players to unite for cooperative challenges or engage in thrilling player-versus-player battles.

As FromSoftware envisioned this grand adventure, they sought to create an open-world experience that echoed the gameplay of Dark Souls, aiming for "Elden Ring" to represent an evolution of the series’ legacy. Miyazaki, inspired by Martin's storytelling prowess, hoped that their collaboration would yield a narrative that felt more accessible than the intricate tales woven in the company's earlier titles. Thus, a new chapter in gaming history began, inviting players into a world teeming with mystery, challenge, and the promise of glory.

On February 13, 2022, anticipation filled the air as gamers eagerly awaited the release of Elden Ring, set to debut on February 25, 2022. This highly anticipated title was poised to make its mark on various platforms, including the PS4, PS5, Xbox One, Xbox Series X, and PC. For those looking to secure their copy ahead of time, pre-orders were available for Elden Ring on the Xbox One, promising an exciting adventure just around the corner.
negative: At that year’s E3, excitement buzzed in the air as FromSoftware unveiled their ambitious project, Elden Ring, touted as their “largest game to date.” Set against a backdrop of a sprawling realm rich with a bloody history, it was a collaboration between the visionary Hidetaka Miyazaki, creator of Dark Souls, and the renowned George R.R. Martin, known for his work on Game of Thrones. Miyazaki spoke passionately about Elden Ring, promising a game brimming with features that had eluded the Dark Souls series. He revealed that this new title would delve deeper into RPG elements, offering players a diverse array of weapons, magic, and combat strategies tailored to their individual styles. As the months rolled by, fans of FromSoftware found themselves yearning for news, their anticipation turning into a creative frenzy as they began crafting their own lore to fill the silence. Excitingly, Elden Ring was set to arrive on PlayStation 4, PlayStation 5, Windows PC, Xbox One, and Xbox Series X the following year, igniting hope and enthusiasm in the gaming community.

In a world where every product was meticulously chosen by an Esquire editor, the promise of earning a commission from those carefully selected links loomed enticingly. As I prepared to indulge myself, I felt the urge to gorge, readying for another 90 hours of blissful hibernation. Just when I thought I had found a semblance of balance in my life—sleeping eight hours a night, enjoying cozy moments with my cat, and even squeezing in the occasional workout—news broke that sent my plans spiraling. The Elden Ring DLC was on its way. The game, which had garnered nearly universal acclaim as the Game of the Year in 2022—at least in the halls of Esquire—was set to make its grand return, promising new and inventive ways to test my limits. This revelation came about a year after the game first captivated players. In this latest chapter, I would take on the role of the "Tarnished," a shadowy figure emerging from exile to either redeem or further plunge a decaying, hellish realm into chaos. Many gamers, weary yet exhilarated, would attest to Elden Ring’s brutal difficulty, a challenge that could devour days of dedication just to conquer the initial bosses. Now, it seemed, we were bracing ourselves for even more trials. On the Elden Ring Japan website, a blog post confirmed the whispers of this impending adventure. I turned to Google Translate, curious yet perplexed by its cryptic message: What in the George R. R. Martin procrastinblogging hell does that mean? Meanwhile, the savvy gamers at Kotaku speculated about the figure seen riding Torrent—the Bullseye to my Jesse in Elden Ring—suggesting that it might just be Miquella himself.

In the dim corridors of anticipation, a buzz had been building, for FromSoftware was on the brink of unveiling Elden Ring, a title that had captured the hearts and minds of gamers everywhere as the much-anticipated successor to the legendary Dark Souls, Bloodborne, and Sekiro. The journey of Elden Ring from the shadows of development to the bright lights of store shelves had almost taken on a life of its own, morphing into a meme that echoed through forums and social media alike. It all began back at E3 2019, where whispers of the game first surfaced, but as the months drifted by, the silence was deafening. Only a few tantalizing pieces of key art and the thrilling announcement of a collaboration with the renowned George R. R. Martin had been offered to whet the appetite of eager fans. 

Now, after what felt like an eternity, the moment was finally drawing near. The developers at FromSoftware, known for their mastery in crafting dark, immersive worlds, along with their publisher Bandai Namco, had begun to peel back the layers of mystery, revealing glimpses of what lay ahead. Hidetaka Miyazaki, the visionary director behind the studio's celebrated titles, spoke with fervor about the game’s rich tapestry. “With Elden Ring, we have applied all our dark fantasy and action-RPG expertise cultivated throughout the Dark Souls series, in order to create a bold, classical evolution of the genre,” he shared, his eyes gleaming with passion. He painted a picture of a vast world, one steeped in legend and peril, crafted from the very imagination of Martin himself. “Elden Ring is a world full of mystery and peril, ready to be explored and discovered; a drama in which various characters flaunt their own mystique and ulterior motives. We sincerely hope you enjoy experiencing it for yourself." The stage was set, and soon, players would step into the enigmatic realm of Elden Ring, ready to forge their own destinies.

As the sun set over the Lands Between, whispers of anticipation filled the air among the Elden Ring faithful. The reveal of the Shadow of the Erdtree release date had become a topic of fervent speculation, igniting the imaginations of players eager to uncover what adventures lay ahead. Christian Vaz, with keen insight, documented this growing excitement on March 1, 2023. 

From the moment they ascended to the throne, embracing their roles as Elden Lords, fans had yearned for more—more battles, more lore, more time spent in the breathtaking world crafted by FromSoftware. While many had lost themselves in the sprawling landscapes of Elden Ring for hundreds of hours, the most dedicated souls had devoted months to dreaming of an expansion that would deepen their journey. 

As one of the premier titles in the realm of PC gaming, Elden Ring’s community was poised on the edge of their seats, eagerly awaiting the announcement of a significant event. The developers had a prime opportunity to unveil the much-anticipated DLC during The Game Awards 2022, where they basked in the glory of winning the prestigious Game of the Year award. Shortly after, as Elden Ring marked its first anniversary, a sense of urgency gripped the fans when the developers hinted at their efforts on the expansion, though they remained tight-lipped about specifics.

Now, with a mystery still shrouding the Elden Ring Shadow of the Erdtree release date, excitement mingled with uncertainty. As murmurs echoed through the community, the prevailing hope was that the DLC would grace the gaming world in 2024, offering a glimpse into what lay beyond the horizon.

In the realm of gaming, anticipation had reached a fever pitch as news of Elden Ring began to circulate. This long-awaited creation, born from the minds at FromSoftware and the storytelling prowess of George R.R. Martin, was finally stepping into the light in 2022. The excitement was palpable, especially when Bandai Namco unveiled a fresh glimpse of this epic fantasy role-playing game during the Summer Game Fest Kickoff Live stream on a Thursday evening. 

As the new trailer played, viewers were treated to gameplay that felt like a familiar embrace for those who had journeyed through the treacherous worlds of Dark Souls, Bloodborne, and Sekiro: Shadows Die Twice. The release date was announced, sending waves of joy through the community: January 21, 2022. Gamers would be able to experience Elden Ring on both current and last-gen consoles, as well as on PC. 

What awaited players was a landscape rich with elements that were sure to resonate with Dark Souls fans—swords and sorcery intertwined with summonable allies and colossal mythical beasts waiting to be vanquished. Yet, there was an exciting twist: the inclusion of horses, a feature that promised to add a new dimension to the gameplay. As the anticipation grew, new 4K images of Elden Ring were revealed, inviting players to immerse themselves in this fantastical world. The journey to this moment had been long; the last whispers of Elden Ring had echoed in June 2019, when Bandai Namco and FromSoftware first unveiled their ambitious project.

Miyazaki took the helm, guiding the vision for Elden Ring’s mechanics, art, and music, weaving together a rich tapestry of gameplay elements. The design team, inspired by vast environmental scales, immersive roleplaying, and compelling storytelling, set out to craft an experience unlike any other. They believed that the expansive scale would foster a sense of diversity, allowing players to engage with the world in myriad ways. To achieve this, they constructed numerous explorable structures, seamlessly integrating them into the open world. Miyazaki drew upon a wealth of influences, naming titles like Shadow of the Colossus, The Elder Scrolls, The Witcher 3, and The Legend of Zelda: Breath of the Wild as key inspirations for the game’s design. Additionally, he looked to the table-top RPG RuneQuest and the legendary works of The Lord of the Rings and The Eternal Champion to shape the narrative. Meanwhile, a talented group of composers—Tsukasa Saitoh, Shoi Miyazawa, Tai Tomisawa, Yuka Kitamura, and Yoshimi Kudo—crafted the original soundtrack that would accompany players on their journey. The world first caught the public's eye during an Xbox games conference at E3 2019, sparking excitement and speculation. Prior to this grand reveal, whispers of the game had escaped into the online ether, thanks to a breach in the servers at Bandai Namco Entertainment. The anticipation for Elden Ring soared, yet it wasn’t until June 2021 that a trailer finally unveiled glimpses of the adventure that awaited players.

In a desolate corner of the world, Miquella’s Haligtree languished, its once-vibrant life extinguished now that his protective cocoon had vanished. The great tree, stripped of its vitality, stood as a mere husk, guarded by the remnants of soldiers who clung to their duty amidst the decay. At the foot of this sorrowful monument, Melania awaited the return of her brother, her heart heavy with the silence that echoed from Mohgwyn Palace, where he remained unresponsive. 

As whispers of the Elden Ring Shadow of the Erdtree release date faded into the background, adventurers were encouraged to revisit the rich tapestry of Elden Ring's endings, a reminder of the epic journey they had undertaken. Many pondered the prospect of forging new paths, considering the best builds for fresh characters before the expansion’s arrival.

In West London, a young man named Christian Vaz found solace in these virtual realms. Born and raised amidst the hustle of city life, he spent his days immersed in games like Elden Ring and Genshin Impact, tirelessly pursuing victories in Warzone and perfecting his skills in Street Fighter 6, climbing ever higher on the leaderboards.

Elden Ring had embarked on an extraordinary journey, selling over 13.4 million copies since its launch. Each passing day saw new fans drawn into its expansive world. Since its debut in February, the game had become a near constant presence on the Steam charts, making it difficult for anyone to find a more worthy contender for Game of the Year than FromSoftware’s latest action RPG. As players delved into its depths again and again—after all, there were six endings to discover—the community buzzed with anticipation for what the developers would conjure next, particularly in terms of additional post-launch content. Yet, amid the excitement, a veil of uncertainty hung over the future of the game, as both FromSoftware and their publisher, Bandai Namco, had refrained from providing any definitive answers. Still, those familiar with FromSoftware's legacy knew that the studio had a penchant for delivering downloadable content for their previous titles. Games like Dark Souls 1-3, Bloodborne, and Sekiro: Shadows Die Twice had all enjoyed substantial post-launch support and updates. Whispers of what might come next floated through press releases, one notably featuring a quote from Bandai Namco Entertainment President and CEO Yasuo Miyakawa, hinting at “expanding the brand beyond the game itself.” It was clear that Bandai Namco recognized the need to capitalize on a phenomenon as monumental as Elden Ring, and one could easily imagine that crafting additional content would be a natural path forward.

In a moment that sent ripples of excitement through the gaming community, FromSoftware unveiled the much-anticipated Elden Ring DLC with a single, evocative tweet. Accompanied by a striking image, the message beckoned: “Rise, Tarnished, and let us walk a new path together. An upcoming expansion for Elden Ring, Shadow of the Erdtree, is currently in development. We hope you look forward to new adventures in the Lands Between.” 

A keen-eyed Redditor pointed out that the image featured Miquella, the twin brother of Malenia, gracefully riding Torrent across an unfamiliar field. This landscape diverged from the ones players had come to know in The Lands Between, hinting that the tale of Shadow of the Erdtree might unfold in a bygone era or perhaps even in an alternate realm entirely. Despite the fierce battles players faced against Malenia, one of the game’s most formidable bosses, Miquella had remained a figure shrouded in mystery, never seen in his original form.

FromSoftware was no stranger to the concept of exploring the past. Their first and only Dark Souls DLC, Artorias of the Abyss, had transported players centuries back to confront Manus, Father of the Abyss. While the mechanics of time travel had not yet graced the world of Elden Ring, the prospect loomed on the horizon, tantalizing players with the possibility of what lay ahead. Whispers of the expansion hinted that it might even delve into the depths of Miquella’s dreams, offering a journey unlike any other.

In the world of game development, FromSoftware had carved out a distinct approach, one that often left players piecing together the narrative themselves. This time, the story of their newest creation was intentionally vague, a puzzle waiting to be unraveled through flavor text and optional conversations with non-player characters. Hidetaka Miyazaki, the mastermind behind the project, took particular pleasure in crafting these NPCs with greater depth. He believed that their complexity made them far more intriguing than those from his earlier titles.

In a revealing interview with IGN, Miyazaki shared his decision to entrust George R.R. Martin with the backstory of the game. He explained that the storytelling constraints imposed by FromSoftware's style often limited writers, steering the narrative away from a linear path. By allowing Martin the freedom to develop a rich backstory that players wouldn’t directly engage with, he opened the door for Martin to contribute without the usual restrictions.

The creative team was bolstered by some staff from Game of Thrones, who lent their expertise to this ambitious project. The journey to create Elden Ring began in early 2017, right after the release of The Ringed City, a downloadable content for Dark Souls III. As the game took shape, it was developed alongside Sekiro: Shadows Die Twice, another title under Miyazaki's direction. He noted that while players might find similarities in combat mechanics between Elden Ring and Sekiro, neither game was a direct inspiration for the other. Both titles were developed simultaneously, utilizing a co-director structure that allowed dedicated staff members to guide each game through its initial stages of creation.

In the realm of gaming, Elden Ring emerged as a phenomenon, captivating millions with its intricate world and gameplay. Yet, as reviewers delved into its narrative, a curious observation arose: the story seemed to lack the distinctive touch of George R. R. Martin’s writing. Kyle Orland from Ars Technica remarked on the storytelling's "characteristically sparse and cryptic" nature, suggesting it diverged from what fans of Martin might have anticipated. Meanwhile, Chris Carter of Destructoid found the story to be "low key," although he acknowledged it was better crafted than those of previous titles by FromSoftware.

The marketing campaign, heavily featuring Martin, left some scratching their heads; Aoife Wilson from Eurogamer described it as "baffling," given the ambiguity surrounding his contributions to the narrative. Yet, not all were disheartened by the lack of Martin’s signature style. Mitchell Saltzman expressed his enjoyment of the game, stating that it was the side-stories, rather than a grand narrative, that kept him "enthralled."

As the world embraced Elden Ring, its success was undeniable. By the end of March 2022, it had sold an impressive 13.4 million copies, and that number swelled to 20 million by February 2023. The game reigned as the best-seller across various regions from February to March 2022, claiming the title of the fastest-selling Bandai Namco game ever. In the competitive landscape of 2022, it was the second-bestselling game in the United States, trailing only Call of Duty: Modern Warfare II, while also securing the third spot in Europe and the tenth in Japan at retail. With numerous awards and accolades adorning its achievements, Elden Ring solidified its place in gaming history.

In the swirling excitement of the gaming world, a noteworthy development emerged: Elden Ring’s publisher, Bandai Namco, had been announced as one of the partners for the upcoming Summer Game Fest. The air buzzed with speculation—could this be the moment fans had been waiting for? The prospect of an Elden Ring DLC announcement loomed tantalizingly close, and hopes soared high. Yet, as the clock ticked on and no official word came from either the publisher or the developer regarding any downloadable content, the uncertainty cast a shadow over the timeline for a potential release. 

Reflecting on the past, one could draw parallels to FromSoftware’s previous DLC releases, which offered a glimmer of hope. Historically, the studio had a pattern of launching DLC within a year of a game’s debut, suggesting that the window for excitement might not be too far off. Although no rigid schedule existed, and the unpredictable nature of game development remained, it seemed unlikely that Bandai Namco would choose to delay the release of additional content for too long. After all, the game’s phenomenal success in 2022 demanded attention. 

Yet, caution tempered the enthusiasm; memories lingered of the disappointment surrounding Demon’s Souls, which had seen no post-launch DLC content. As gamers held their breath, they awaited updates, knowing that FromSoftware typically did not adhere to a fixed number of DLC drops. The first Dark Souls game had delivered one DLC, while both Dark Souls 2 and 3 had offered two each. The future remained uncertain, but the community was united in their hope for an adventure yet to unfold.

On a remarkable day in the world of gaming, Kotaku unveiled a captivating story about an environmental artist named Eugenia Lysa. She had embarked on an ambitious journey, crafting her own fanmade DLC for the beloved game Elden Ring using the powerful Unreal Engine 5. Those intrigued could follow the link to delve deeper into her creative process, which included a brief interview that provided a glimpse into Lysa’s artistic vision. 

Meanwhile, the excitement surrounding Elden Ring continued to grow, with Bandai Namco revealing that the game had shipped an impressive 16.6 million copies worldwide by the end of June, according to their latest sales report shared by Gamebiz.jp. With such remarkable success, many speculated that the long-awaited DLC was just around the corner, especially with Gamescom approaching on the horizon. Bandai Namco Entertainment had already confirmed its presence at the event, scheduled to take place from August 23 to 28. The gaming community buzzed with anticipation—could this be the moment when information about the Elden Ring DLC would finally emerge? 

Hope remained high, particularly as Geoff Keighley’s Opening Night Live event was set to kick off the festivities the evening before Gamescom began, and fans were eager for any announcements. Yet, amidst all this speculation, a surprising twist unfolded. While the gaming world held its breath for news of Elden Ring’s DLC, director Hidetaka Miyazaki shared that From Software’s next project was nearing completion. In a recent interview with the Japanese site 4Gamer, he took a moment to clarify remarks he had made back in 2018, leaving fans both curious and excited about what lay ahead.

In a world where creativity reigned supreme, Martin was entrusted with the boundless freedom to craft a rich backstory, while the renowned Miyazaki took the helm as the lead writer for the in-game narrative. Together, they wove a tale that captivated the imagination. The developers poured their hearts into building an expansive environment, focusing on the vastness of the world, the depth of roleplaying, and the intricacies of the story. This ambitious scale necessitated the construction of numerous structures throughout the open world, each a testament to their vision. 

Upon its release, Elden Ring was met with resounding acclaim, celebrated for its sprawling open world, engaging gameplay systems, and intricate setting, though some voices raised concerns about its technical performance. It swiftly garnered multiple Game of the Year awards, its popularity soaring as it sold over 20 million copies in just a year. In February 2023, excitement rippled through the gaming community with the announcement of an expansion titled Shadow of the Erdtree.

Elden Ring invites players into a third-person perspective, action role-playing experience that emphasizes both combat and exploration. Echoes of other FromSoftware classics—like the Dark Souls series, Bloodborne, and Sekiro: Shadows Die Twice—can be felt throughout its design. Set against the backdrop of the Lands Between, players are free to traverse its sprawling landscapes, including the serene Limgrave with its grassy plains and ancient ruins, and the desolate Caelid, a wasteland teeming with undead monsters.

As players journey through this open world, they ride upon their trusty mount, Torrent, the wind at their backs, or opt for the convenience of fast travel when the situation allows. Along the way, they encounter a myriad of non-player characters and formidable foes, including the demigods who wield dominion over each region, standing as the primary adversaries in this epic saga.

As the clock ticked down to the highly anticipated airing of the Elden Ring segment at The Game Awards, just under two weeks away, a question began to circulate among fans: what World Premiere were they yearning to see the most? To many, the answer was not a newly announced title or a speculative sequel, but rather something more immediate—a DLC for the beloved Elden Ring, which had emerged as the frontrunner for Game of the Year this year. The idea of unveiling additional content for Elden Ring during the awards show, especially as it stood a strong chance of clinching the coveted Game of the Year title, felt like a perfect match. 

With Elden Ring having made its grand debut in late February 2022, the early December timing would mark just over nine months since its release. Fans noted that this delay was unusual, especially in comparison to the timelines of previous FromSoftware games. In the past, titles like Dark Souls 2 and 3, along with Bloodborne, had already released their DLC by this point. The Old Hunters DLC for Bloodborne, for instance, was announced a mere six months after its launch, with a subsequent release just two months later. Dark Souls 2’s DLC announcement came just three months after that game hit the shelves. 

So, what was causing the wait for Elden Ring’s DLC? The answer, as many speculated, lay in the lingering shadows of the COVID pandemic, which had cast a long pall over the gaming industry. The year 2022 had turned out to be relatively sparse for new game releases, with a few notable exceptions—Elden Ring being one of them.

As time went on, it became clear that a little extra patience was warranted. The world of Elden Ring was vast and captivating, a sensation that eclipsed anything else that FromSoftware had ever unveiled. There was truly no need to rush; the game had solidified its position as a monumental best-seller. Whether they chose to release downloadable content tomorrow or six months from now, it was certain to continue its impressive sales trajectory. Perhaps the timing had been orchestrated with remarkable precision. One could envision the thrilling moment when Elden Ring was awarded Game of the Year, its long-awaited DLC announcement echoing through the halls of the Game Awards. Such a spectacle would undoubtedly ignite a wave of pre-sales, drawing millions in anticipation.

In the back of my mind, I pondered the nature of this DLC. Given the expansive world of Elden Ring, it seemed reasonable to expect that the forthcoming content would be more intricate and extensive than any previous offerings from FromSoftware. Yet, a flicker of doubt lingered; with the recent trend away from DLC for major titles—like GTA 5, which, despite its monumental success, never released paid single-player story content—there was a possibility they might forgo DLC altogether in favor of diving straight into their next project. Still, the signs pointed towards the likelihood of an announcement, and the timing felt just right.

As the excitement built, I found myself compelled to share my thoughts with others. The world of social media beckoned—Twitter, YouTube, Facebook, and Instagram. And I couldn’t resist inviting them to subscribe to my free weekly content round-up newsletter, God Rolls. Meanwhile, my own creative endeavors awaited, with the Herokiller series and The Earthborn Trilogy ready to capture the imaginations of readers everywhere.

In a recent conversation, Miyazaki revealed an intriguing detail about the development progress of his team, mentioning they had "three and a half" games underway. He elaborated, stating that "0.5" referred to Déraciné, "1.0" was Sekiro, and the remaining "2.0" consisted of titles yet to be announced. Little did they know, one of those hidden gems would soon be known as Elden Ring. Curious about the fate of the other project, 4Gamer pressed him for answers, and Miyazaki replied with assurance: "Yes, it is. We are in the final stages of development." 

As whispers of excitement filled the air, speculation grew that this new game might make its grand debut at The Game Awards later this year. In the heart of Beijing, China, from February 4 to 20, 2022, the XXIV Olympic Winter Games unfolded, bringing together a remarkable gathering of athletic prowess. Among them, Norway shone brightly, dominating the medal count with an impressive 37 medals, including a record-breaking 16 golds—the highest achieved at a single Winter Games. The host nation, China, also made history, surpassing its previous gold medal tally of five from the 2010 Winter Olympics by securing nine golds this time around. With a total of 15 medals, China's performance eclipsed its earlier records from both the 2006 and 2010 Winter Games.

As the events unfolded, certain athletes etched their names into the annals of Olympic history. Biathletes Johannes Thingnes Bø, Quentin Fillon Maillet, and Marte Olsbu Røiseland, along with cross-country skier Alexander Bolshunov, each dazzled spectators, claiming five medals apiece. Bø, in a remarkable feat, also captured four gold medals, showcasing his extraordinary talent. Meanwhile, New Zealand celebrated a historic moment as snowboarder Zoi Sadowski-Synnott earned the nation’s first Winter Olympic gold medal, a triumph that would be remembered for years to come.

In the grand tapestry of the 2022 Winter Games, Sweden stood proudly with 18 medals, comprising eight gleaming golds, five silvers, and five bronzes. Close on its heels was Austria, also with 18 medals, but a different distribution: seven golds, seven silvers, and four bronzes. Japan, too, found its place in the spotlight with 18 medals, though its tally included three golds, six silvers, and nine bronzes. The Netherlands followed, collecting 17 medals—eight of which were gold, alongside five silvers and four bronzes. Italy, with its own total of 17 medals, showcased two golds, seven silvers, and eight bronzes. For those keen to delve into the complete final medal count, NBCOlympics.com was the place to visit. 

Yet, it was Norway that truly reigned supreme, not only emerging with the highest medal count but also capturing the most coveted golds, totaling an impressive 16. Among the athletes, one stood out above the rest: Johannes Thingnes Bo. This remarkable competitor dominated the men’s biathlon events, claiming four gold medals by triumphing in the sprint, mass start, relay, and mixed relay. At just 28 years old, Bo now boasts five Olympic golds, having previously secured a first-place finish in the individual biathlon event during the 2018 Winter Games. Norway’s glory didn’t end there; two other athletes also shone brightly, each earning three gold medals—Marte Olsbu Roiseland in women’s biathlon and Therese Johaug in women’s cross-country skiing.

In the heart of PyeongChang, a remarkable chapter in Olympic history unfolded as Norway showcased its dominance in the Winter Games. With a staggering total of 368 medals, the nation celebrated an extraordinary triumph, securing 39 medals in total during this prestigious event. Among these, 14 gleamed with the brilliance of gold, seven of which were earned in the nation’s beloved sport of cross country skiing, where they achieved a staggering 121 medals over the years. 

As the games progressed, Norway's collection grew to include 14 silver medals and 11 bronze medals, solidifying its place at the top of the medal table. This achievement marked the eighth occasion Norway had led the final medal count at a Winter Olympics, surpassing the Soviet Union and setting a new record for the most victories in history. Many of these triumphs came in the early days of the Winter Olympics, with Norway winning the most medals in three of the first four editions. The last time the country had triumphed in such a manner was in 1994, when it hosted the Winter Olympics in Lillehammer. 

Not only did Norway shine in PyeongChang, but the nation also held the distinction of being the all-time leader in medals across three Winter Olympic sports: cross country skiing, ski jumping, and Nordic combined, with 35 and 31 medals respectively. The echoes of victory resonated through the mountains and valleys of Norway, a testament to their unwavering dedication and prowess in winter sports.

In a remarkable display of athletic prowess, China achieved a total of 15 medals at the Games, including an impressive nine golds. Among the standout athletes was Eileen Gu, an 18-year-old freeskier with dual heritage, who, although residing in the U.S., proudly competed for China. She dazzled spectators by clinching two gold medals in freeski big air and freeski halfpipe, along with a silver in freeski slopestyle. Another notable competitor was snowboarder Su Yiming, who also shone brightly, securing gold in men’s big air and silver in slopestyle. This success was no accident; it was the culmination of China’s substantial investments in its ski and snowboard programs, as well as winter sports tourism, following the awarding of the Beijing Games in 2015. As the curtain fell on the competition, the total medal count for Norway reached an impressive 25, with eight shining golds among them. The anticipated performance didn’t quite match expectations, yet by the end of the Games, the U.S. had amassed a total of 25 medals, including eight golds. 

Mikaela Shiffrin, a decorated athlete with three Olympic medals to her name, found herself amidst the pressure of competition, participating in five individual Alpine skiing events but left without a medal in this particular Games. The U.S. mixed team event on that Saturday night, which was Sunday morning in China, ended with a fourth-place finish—just shy of the podium.

However, freestyle skiing emerged as the shining star for the United States, with athletes claiming eight medals and two golden victories, thanks to Alex Hall in slopestyle and the mixed team aerials. Additional gold medals were secured by the U.S. squad, including Kallie Humphries, who transitioned from Team Canada to triumph in bobsled, Nathan Chen in figure skating, Lindsey Jacobellis in snowboard cross, Chloe Kim in snowboard halfpipe, Erin Jackson in speedskating, and the talented mixed team in snowboard cross. The stories of competition, triumph, and resilience echoed through the snowy peaks and icy arenas, weaving a memorable tapestry of the Games.

In the frosty embrace of the Winter Games, the Norwegians stood as giants, their prowess shining through a remarkable array of gold medals won in biathlon, cross-country skiing, freestyle skiing, Nordic combined, ski jumping, and speed skating. With each event, they showcased their dominance, leaving no doubt that Norway was the force to be reckoned with once again. 

Behind them, Germany carved its own path, securing third place in total medals, trailing only Norway and the Russian Olympic Committee. Yet, the Germans had a secret weapon—twice as many gold medals as the ROC, a total of twelve shimmering victories. The bobsled and luge events became their playground, where speed met precision and teamwork flourished. 

Among the stars of the German team was Francesco Friedrich, whose name resonated throughout the arenas. Alongside Thorsten Margis, he clinched two golds in bobsled, a feat that echoed with triumph. In the luge, Johannes Ludwig and the dynamic duo of Natalie Geisenberger and the Tobias team—Arlt and Wendl—each celebrated their own golden moments, some in thrilling doubles and relays.

Friedrich's journey was particularly extraordinary. For the second consecutive Winter Olympics, he emerged unscathed, winning gold in both the two-man and four-man bobsled events. Tim Reynolds of the Associated Press noted that Friedrich had etched his name in Olympic history, becoming the first bobsledder to achieve this remarkable feat at back-to-back games. His success was not just personal; it fueled the fire of Germany’s overall triumph, solidifying their impressive showing in the icy theater of competition.

The 2022 Games in Beijing witnessed a slight dip from the record-breaking 30 National Olympic Committees that found glory in PyeongChang, yet still, 29 different nations celebrated their triumphs. Reflecting on the journey since the inaugural Winter Olympics in 1924, where only 16 events across six sports unfolded in Chamonix, France, one can only marvel at the evolution of this global event. It’s worth noting that the ROC's gold in team figure skating remains provisional, hinging on the outcome of Kamila Valieva's doping case. The United States finished strong in second place, while Japan and Canada followed close behind in third and fourth, respectively.

As the anticipation built for the 2022 Winter Olympics, athletes from every corner of the globe prepared for the chance to hear their nation's anthem resonate in the air following their hard-fought victories. History hinted at the familiar tunes that would echo through the arenas, as crowds eagerly awaited the melodies of champions. The Winter Olympics had long been a stage dominated by European nations, with the United States and Canada also vying for glory as the competition unfolded. The countries that once stood atop the podium during the inaugural Winter Games in 1924 had managed to maintain their relevance over the decades, while others experienced their own peaks of success before being reshaped by the tides of history into new nations. Among the established European powers and the two North American giants, a question loomed: which nation held the record for the highest medal count of all time? 

As the 2022 Winter Games approached, the spotlight turned to the ten countries that had amassed the most medals in Winter Olympics history. The totals painted a picture of athletic prowess: 130 medals in all, with 45 gold, 44 silver, and 41 bronze. The thrilling action of the Beijing Olympics would unfold live on NBC, showcasing the incredible talent on display. Notably, the sport that had yielded the most medals was speed skating, with a remarkable 121 awards claimed by Dutch athletes, who were known for gliding across the ice in their signature orange uniforms, often departing with gold medals in hand. This impressive tally stood 37 medals ahead of any other country in the discipline and was nearly double the Netherlands' achievements in its premier summer sport, swimming, which had garnered 62 medals.

In the grand tapestry of the Winter Olympics, the country proudly boasts a remarkable collection of 18 alpine skiing medals, among them two shining golds earned at PyeongChang. The tally continues with 16 medals in speed skating, 14 in biathlon, 11 in ice hockey, and 10 in figure skating, showcasing a diverse strength across winter sports. During the 2018 games in PyeongChang, Sweden emerged as a formidable competitor, securing seven gold medals and finishing sixth overall among the participating nations. Their total medal count of 14 placed them jointly in 10th position. 

As the tally stands, Sweden has amassed a total of 167 medals, with 43 gold, 63 silver, and 61 bronze. Meanwhile, the sport that stands out the most for Sweden is cross-country skiing, where they have claimed an impressive 80 medals. Not to be overshadowed, Finland shares a parallel passion for cross-country skiing, also boasting 80 medals in this discipline. Following closely are their achievements in speed skating with 24 medals, ski jumping with 22, and Nordic combined with 14 medals.

However, the 2018 Winter Olympics proved to be a challenging endeavor for Finland. They returned home with the smallest medal haul of all, securing just six medals, one of which was gold. Notably, four of these medals, including the coveted gold, were earned in the realm of cross-country skiing. Despite their seventh-place standing in the medal count, Finland's modest collection is not a rarity; they remain the sole nation among the top ten that has never exceeded 15 medals at a single Winter Olympics.

In the world of winter sports, Germany's prowess is undeniable. The nation boasts a remarkable collection of medals across various disciplines, including ski jumping, where it has secured 25 medals, luge with 22, and figure skating, which adds another 20 to the tally. Altogether, Germany has amassed a staggering total of 259 medals, comprising 100 gold, 94 silver, and 65 bronze. The 2018 Winter Olympics in PyeongChang marked a significant chapter in this narrative, with Germany achieving 31 medals, the second highest of any nation, and tying for first with 14 golds.

Germany's legacy in the Winter Olympics is further highlighted by its repeated successes; it stands as one of only three countries to lead in medal counts four times, in the years 1992, 1998, 2002, and 2006. The story of East Germany adds another layer to this rich history, as it consistently ranked among the top two in medal counts from 1972 to 1988, including a standout performance in 1980, where it led with 23 medals. 

As we reflect on the achievements in sports like biathlon and luge, Germany emerges as a leader, having claimed 37 medals in each of these events. When combined with the contributions from East Germany, which accounted for 29 medals, West Germany's 10, and the United Team of Germany's 5, the combined total represents an impressive 57.4% of all medals ever awarded in these sports. This narrative of dominance continues to unfold, celebrating Germany's enduring legacy in the Winter Olympics.

From 1994 to 2014, Russia proudly amassed a total of 121 medals, but the winds of change began to blow when the International Olympic Committee decided to strip the nation of its hard-earned accolades from the 2014 Sochi Games. This action left the exact count of Russia’s medals in a state of flux, dependent on the IOC’s future decisions regarding any further reallocations. In 2018, a new chapter unfolded as Russian athletes took to the stage under the banner of the Russian Olympic Committee (ROC), where they showcased their talent and secured 17 medals. They are set to compete under the same designation in the upcoming Beijing Games. If one were to tally the medals from these three significant periods, Russia would find itself in second place on the all-time medal list, boasting a total of 232 medals: 64 gold, 81 silver, and 87 bronze.

Meanwhile, Austria has carved its name into the annals of alpine skiing history, dominating the sport with an impressive 121 medals, which represent more than half of its overall Olympic achievements (52.2%) and account for 26.2% of all medals awarded in alpine skiing at the Olympics. During the PyeongChang Games, Austria's strength shone brightly, as seven of its 14 medals were claimed in alpine skiing, allowing the nation to tie with Switzerland for the top spot, each securing seven medals in this fiercely competitive discipline. In addition to their alpine success, Austria also added three medals in luge, one in biathlon, one in Nordic combined, and one in snowboarding, further solidifying its prowess in winter sports.

In the grand tapestry of Olympic history, Team USA’s journey through the Winter Games has been a remarkable tale of resilience and achievement. With a total of 305 medals to its name—105 of which are gold, 112 silver, and 88 bronze—the nation has etched its presence on the icy fields of competition. At the PyeongChang Olympics, Team USA secured 23 medals, showcasing their prowess, particularly in speed skating, where they amassed an impressive 68 medals. 

Yet, despite these accomplishments, the team’s Winter Olympic success pales in comparison to its dominance in the Summer Games, where they boast more than double the medals of their closest rival. The 21st century has marked a strong resurgence for the U.S. in the Winter Olympics, as they claimed three second-place finishes in overall medal counts from 2002 through 2014, even standing atop the podium with a staggering 37 medals at the 2010 Vancouver Olympics. In PyeongChang, however, they found themselves in fourth place, garnering 23 medals once again.

Reflecting on the past two decades, it is clear that the U.S. has experienced a revitalizing return to form on the Winter Olympic stage. The 20th century offered only a single moment of glory at the top of the medal table, back in 1932, when Lake Placid hosted its inaugural Winter Games. Today, Team USA proudly leads in two sports, yet ironically, speed skating—where they have garnered the most medals—does not hold the title of their top sport. Instead, they shine brightest in figure skating, where they have claimed 51 medals, and snowboarding, with a remarkable 31 medals to their credit.

In the grand tally of winter sports, the numbers tell a compelling story. Canada, standing tall with a total of 199 medals, shines brightly with 73 gold, 64 silver, and 62 bronze. Their prowess was particularly evident in PyeongChang, where they secured two medals, but their dominance is notably concentrated in team sports. The nation proudly claims the title of all-time leader in Winter Olympic history for ice hockey, with an impressive 25 medals, and curling, where they have amassed 11. With only five events shared between these two sports—each featuring both men's and women's tournaments, along with the newly introduced mixed doubles curling tournament—the competition for hardware is fierce. Despite this, Canada’s accomplishments extend beyond team sports; they have higher medal totals in individual disciplines such as speed skating, where they have tallied 37 medals, short track speed skating with 33, figure skating at 29, and freestyle skiing, where they are tied with the U.S. at 25 medals.

Across the historical landscape of the Winter Olympics, the Soviet Union made its debut in 1956, representing a collection of independent nations. This legacy continued as the former republics united as the Unified Team, competing together in both the 1992 Winter and Summer Olympics before returning to their individual identities at the 1994 Winter Games. Their combined efforts have contributed significantly to a remarkable total of 217 medals, including 87 gold, 63 silver, and 67 bronze, with cross country skiing standing out as the sport with the most accolades, boasting 77 medals.

As the 2022 Winter Olympics unfolded, China found itself just shy of the top 10 in the overall medal tally, landing in 11th place with a total of 15 medals. Yet, the host nation shone brightly in the gold medal category, securing an impressive nine golds, which placed them third in that regard. Among the standout athletes were freestyle skier Eileen Gu and short-track speed skater Ren Ziwei, both of whom dazzled the crowd by winning two gold medals each. Eileen Gu, in particular, made history; not only did she clinch two golds in the big air and halfpipe events, but she also earned a silver medal, becoming one of only 12 athletes at the Games to achieve three or more medals. At just 18 years old, Gu became the first freestyle skier to accomplish such a feat at a single Winter Games, marking a remarkable milestone in her Olympic journey. While China may not have topped the medal count, its athletes provided the home crowd with plenty of reasons to cheer, showcasing their prowess and determination throughout the thrilling Winter Games.

In the realm of Winter Olympic sports, Switzerland boasts a total of nine medals distributed across three disciplines: short track speed skating, where they earned five medals; figure skating, claiming three; and snowboarding, securing one. Their overall tally stands impressively at 153 medals, comprising 56 gold, 45 silver, and 52 bronze. During the PyeongChang Games, they added 15 more to their collection, with alpine skiing emerging as their most fruitful sport, yielding 66 medals. While Switzerland ranks second on the all-time Olympic medal table for alpine skiing, they still trail significantly behind the leading country in the sport. Remarkably, their achievements in alpine skiing are balanced, with 22 medals of each color—gold, silver, and bronze. 

Following alpine skiing, bobsledding emerges as Switzerland's next best performing Winter Olympic sport, garnering a total of 31 medals—10 gold, 10 silver, and 11 bronze—making them the most successful nation in this discipline. Snowboarding also adds to their accolades, with 13 medals earned. 

Meanwhile, Sweden has carved out its own legacy with 158 total Olympic medals, including 57 gold, 46 silver, and 55 bronze. The PyeongChang Games contributed 14 medals to their count, with cross country skiing being their standout sport, yielding an impressive 80 medals. Sweden finds itself tied for second place in the all-time Olympic cross country skiing medal count with a neighboring nation that follows closely behind. However, aside from cross country skiing, Sweden does not boast another sport that quite matches its prominence.

In a thrilling display of athletic prowess, Germany dominated the men's two-man bobsleigh competition, claiming all three spots on the podium. Francesco Friedrich and Thorsten Margis glided to victory, securing the gold medal, while Johannes Lochner and Florian Bauer followed closely behind to capture the silver. Meanwhile, Christoph Hafer and Matthias Sommer rounded out the trio with a well-earned bronze. The medal standings were meticulously compiled, adhering to the standards set forth by the International Olympic Committee (IOC). By tradition, nations were ranked first by the number of gold medals won, followed by silver and bronze. In a notable twist, two bronze medals were awarded in the freestyle women's ski cross event. As the excitement of the competition filled the air, it was important to remember that the results for the Figure Skating Team and the Women's Single Skating events were still provisional, awaiting final confirmation.

On July 31, 2015, a historic moment unfolded as Beijing triumphantly secured the bid for the 2022 Olympic Winter Games, marking the city as the first in the world to host both summer and winter editions of the prestigious Olympic Games. With a spirited vision dubbed “Joyful Rendezvous upon Pure Ice and Snow,” Beijing 2022 sought to build upon the remarkable legacy of the Beijing 2008 Olympic Games, igniting a passion for winter sports in the hearts of 300 million people across China. 

As the world turned its attention to the Games, a total of 109 events across seven Olympic winter sports were set to take place in three distinct competition zones: the bustling heart of central Beijing, the serene landscapes of Yanqing, and the snowy slopes of Zhangjiakou. In the vibrant cityscape of Beijing, spectators were treated to thrilling performances in four snow events, including the exhilarating snowboard big air and freestyle skiing big air contests for both men and women, alongside all the ice events such as curling, ice hockey, and skating, all utilizing the legacy venues from 2008.

Meanwhile, nestled about 80 kilometers to the northwest, the suburban district of Yanqing, famous for its proximity to the majestic Badaling and Juyongguan stretches of the Great Wall, welcomed athletes competing in Alpine skiing and sliding events, including bobsleigh, skeleton, and luge. Further to the northwest, in the neighboring Hebei province, Zhangjiakou became a hub for snow sports, hosting a variety of events in its picturesque Chongli district—renowned as a popular skiing destination—where snowboard, freestyle skiing, cross country skiing, ski jumping, Nordic combined, and biathlon competitions unfolded against a stunning winter backdrop.

In 2019, the Beijing-Zhangjiakou high-speed railway was completed, transforming travel in the region. Suddenly, the journey from Beijing to Yanqing took a mere 20 minutes, while reaching Zhangjiakou became a swift 50-minute ride. As the world turned its gaze toward Beijing in February 2022 for the Olympic Winter Games, a fresh air of excitement filled the atmosphere. The Games, coinciding with the vibrant Chinese New Year, heralded a new chapter in Olympic history. Seven new events were introduced, including the women's monobob and freestyle skiing big air for both men and women, alongside mixed team events in short track speed skating team relay, ski jumping, freestyle skiing aerials, and snowboard cross. This expansion brought forth the most gender-balanced Winter Games ever, with female athletes making up 45.44 percent of the participants and a record number of women's events. As local residents embraced the influx of athletes from around the world, they encouraged their guests to partake in the festivities of the Spring Festival, showcasing the breathtaking landscapes and the rich cultural tapestry of Beijing, Yanqing, and Zhangjiakou.
